title: Fix DoltQueryService pool-per-call anti-pattern
description:
Fixed twice over:
1. Commit 33866a5 (2026-03-26) 'fix: implement DoltQueryService pool singleton to prevent connection exhaustion' — directly addressed this issue (mentioned by ID in commit body). Added module-level Map cache keyed by gitRepoPath, getOrCreatePool() reuse, cleanupIdlePools() every 60s.
2. Commit 6ee446c (2026-04-14) 'refactor: remove beads runtime and add project archive lifecycle' — deleted lib/DoltQueryService.js entirely when the beads runtime was extracted. The offending code path no longer exists.

title: Create beads data fetcher utility for memory builders
description: Create a shared utility (e.g. BeadsDataFetcher.js) that wraps bd list --all --json or reads from BeadsDBReader/dolt. Returns normalized issue objects that all builder functions can consume. Single source of truth for beads data access in the memory pipeline.

title: Rewire Temporal orchestration-letta.ts to use beads data sources
description: orchestration-letta.ts updateLettaMemory() calls buildBoardMetrics(hulyIssues) and buildProjectMeta(hulyProject, hulyIssues). After the individual builders are migrated, update this caller to fetch beads data (bd list --all --json or BeadsDBReader) and pass it to the new builder signatures.

title: Rewire buildProjectMeta to use ProjectRegistry SQLite
description: buildProjectMeta() takes hulyProject object. Refactor to read from ProjectRegistry SQLite (projects table). Fields: name, identifier, filesystem_path, git_url, status, issue_count. Remove huly.id and huly.identifier fields.

title: Rewire buildRecentActivity to read from beads interactions.jsonl
description: buildRecentActivity() takes Huly activity feed. Refactor to parse .beads/interactions.jsonl for recent issue changes. Extract created/updated/closed events. Preserve pattern detection (blocked_spike, high_activity, completion_streak).

title: Rewire buildBacklogSummary to read from beads instead of Huly
description: buildBacklogSummary() in LettaMemoryBuilders.js filters hulyIssues by Todo/Backlog status. Refactor to filter beads issues by status=open. Sort by beads priority (P0-P4) instead of Huly priority strings.

title: Rewire buildHotspots to read from beads instead of Huly
description: buildHotspots() in LettaMemoryBuilders.js takes hulyIssues[]. Refactor to accept beads issues. Map beads priority (P0-P4) to hotspot detection. Blocked detection via title/description keywords stays the same. Ageing WIP needs beads modified_at timestamps.

title: Rewire buildBoardMetrics to read from beads instead of Huly
description: buildBoardMetrics() in LettaMemoryBuilders.js takes hulyIssues[]. Refactor to accept beads issues from bd list --all --json. Map beads statuses (open/closed) to metric counts. Update Temporal orchestration-letta.ts caller.

title: O(n²) in BeadsBatchSync.js — .find() inside sync loop
description: Lines 112-163: loop over beadsIssues calls syncBeadsIssueToHuly() which internally does .find() on hulyIssues array. 100 beads issues × 100 huly issues = 10,000 comparisons per sync. Fix: pre-build hulyIssueMap (new Map(hulyIssues.map(i => [i.identifier, i]))) and pass it instead of raw array.

title: O(n²) nested loops in BeadsParentChildSync.js
description: Lines 287-305: nested loop with .find() on full beadsIssues array per tree node. Lines 331-334: loop with TWO db.getIssue() calls per iteration. Lines 86, 232, 281: buildIssueIndexByBeadsId() called 3x with same data from db.getAllIssues(). Fix: pre-build beadsIssues Map once (new Map(beadsIssues.map(i => [i.id, i]))), build index once and pass as parameter.

title: Dedupe index rebuilt 3x in huly-dedupe.ts
description: getProjectIssueIndex() builds 3 maps (byBeadsId, byNormalizedTitle, byHulyIdentifier) but is called separately by findMappedIssueByBeadsId(), findMappedIssueByTitle(), getBeadsStatusForHulyIssue() — each opens its own DB connection and rebuilds all 3 maps, only using 1. Cache TTL is 15s but within a single sync cycle the same project is queried 3+ times. Fix: return all 3 maps from a single getProjectIssueIndex() call, cache the entire index.

title: DB connection thrashing in sync-database.ts
description: Each activity function in sync-database.ts (lines 61-226) opens/closes the SQLite DB independently: getIssueSyncTimestamps(), hasBeadsIssueChanged(), getIssueSyncState(), getIssueSyncStateBatch(), persistIssueSyncState(), persistIssueSyncStateBatch(). For 50 projects × 100 issues = 500+ unnecessary DB connections per sync cycle. Fix: pass DB connection as parameter or use connection pooling, batch all DB operations into single transaction.

title: Add vendor/dependency exclusion filters to AST sync pipeline
Add missing vibe_task_id database index for faster task lookups
Fix N+1 query in BeadsParentChildSync — cache db.getAllIssues() before loop
FormulaDispatcher core — walk molecule via RuntimeProvider
close_reason: Added FormulaDispatcher core with serial molecule walk, pack role lookup, prompt rendering, RuntimeProvider dispatch/observe/stop, step failure handling, and dispatcher events; dispatcher tests pass and type-check was run after fixing EventBus layer union.

title: Test scaffolding — FakeProvider fixture + InMemoryDoltClient + MoleculeWalker tests
description:
Foundation slice. Two reusable test doubles + the missing MoleculeWalker unit tests. Blocks .3 (FormulaDispatcher).

## Why

MoleculeWalker.dispatch/findReady/startStep/finishStep/failStep already exist in src/orchestration/molecule/molecule.ts with zero unit tests — tests/unit/orchestration/molecule/ does not exist. The dispatcher (.3) will be the walker's first integration check, which conflates risks. Pull walker testing forward.

DoltClient is required by the walker but binds to a real Dolt instance. The dispatcher needs to be unit-testable without that — hence the InMemoryDoltClient double.

FakeProvider already exists inline in tests/unit/orchestration/runtime/extra-providers.test.ts (makeFakeProvider). Promote + extend so .3 can drive it with a programmable script.

## Scope

- tests/_fixtures/fake-provider.ts: exports `newFakeProvider({ kind?, script? })` returning a RuntimeProvider. The script callback shape is `(spec: SessionSpec) => AsyncIterable<SessionEvent>` so tests can yield arbitrary event sequences (started → message-delta → tool-call → turn-done, or started → error, etc.). Also exposes a recorder so tests can assert what prompt() was called with.
- tests/_fixtures/in-memory-dolt-client.ts: InMemoryDoltClient implementing only the surface MoleculeWalker uses — getBead, getBeadDependencies, insertMoleculeRoot, insertMoleculeStep, findReadyStepsForMolecule, markStepRunning, markStepDone, markStepFailed. Backed by Map<beadId, BeadRow> + DependencyRow[]. No SQL.
- tests/unit/orchestration/molecule/walker.test.ts: covers dispatch creates root+step beads with parent-child + blocks edges; findReady returns the dep-root step first; closing a step unblocks dependents; dependsOn forward-ref validation throws; startStep / finishStep / failStep mark the bead state correctly.

## Done means

bunx vitest run tests/unit/orchestration/molecule passes; ~10 new tests; src/ untouched. The fixtures are imported (not re-defined) by .3's tests.

title: Wire orchestration plane to formula-run CLI + HTTP path
description:
Adoption epic — make the orchestration plane (src/orchestration/*) load-bearing for a real `vibesync formula run code-review` invocation.

## Context

Today the plane is implementation-complete (formula parser, molecule walker, runtime providers, event bus, health patrol, drift auditor, packs) but ZERO callers exist outside its own tests and the smoke script under scripts/smoke/. src/index.ts, src/cli.ts, and src/ApiServer.ts contain no imports from src/orchestration/. The runtime backend works (vibesync-6wn, closed 2026-05-17) but there is no dispatcher walking a molecule through it, no API endpoint, and no CLI verb.

## Scope

Eight journey-shaped slices (children .1-.8). Each independently committable; each adds tests; each does not perturb the existing sync service (orchestration boot is gated on ENABLE_ORCHESTRATION=true).

  1. Test scaffolding (FakeProvider + InMemoryDoltClient + missing walker tests).
  2. Prompt template renderer (${var} interpolation, path-traversal safe).
  3. FormulaDispatcher core — keystone.
  4. Memory blocks for remaining roles (coder/tester/mayor/refinery).
  5. Boot wiring in src/orchestration/boot.ts + src/index.ts.
  6. HTTP routes (POST /formulas/:name/run, GET /molecules/:id, SSE events).
  7. CLI verbs (vibesync formula {list,run,status,events}).
  8. Real-server end-to-end smoke test for code-review.toml.

## Out of scope (each becomes a separate follow-up bead)

Resumability across process restart, parallel ready-step fan-out, mid-run cancellation, retry/backoff, REST-side cleanup of teams' default memory blocks, auth on orchestration routes, concurrent dispatcher rate limits.

## Done means

- bun run start with ENABLE_ORCHESTRATION=true boots the orchestration plane without disturbing the existing sync service.
- vibesync formula run code-review --input "<text>" returns a moleculeId.
- vibesync formula events <moleculeId> streams dispatcher/* events until dispatcher/formula.completed.
- Three step beads (reviewer, coder, tester) land in closed state with non-empty output recorded.
- scripts/smoke/formula-code-review.ts runs green against http://192.168.50.90:8289 (Letta 0.16.7).

title: Wire LettaTeamsProvider.observe() to real task-progress events
description:
Replace the synthetic started→turn-done skeleton in
src/orchestration/runtime/letta-teams-provider.ts with real
SessionEvents derived from letta-teams-sdk's task-visibility
stream.

## Why this is the gamble

Everything downstream (EventBus subscribers, HealthPatrol,
molecule progression, formula step transitions) depends on
observe() emitting useful events. Until it's wired, the rest of
Gastown is blind to what teammates are actually doing.

## Surface to map

letta-teams-sdk exposes task progress via:

- runtime.tasks.wait(taskId, onProgress)
- task-visibility module (per-turn frames as tasks advance)

Map those onto our SessionEvent union — at minimum:
session_started, turn_started, turn_chunk, turn_done, error.

## Done means

- observe() async-iterates over real teams events for a session.
- A vitest case feeds a fake teams runtime that emits a scripted
  progress sequence and asserts the SessionEvents the provider
  yields, in order.
- The molecule + formula layer can drive a single-step code-review
  run against the gastown pack and see real turn boundaries.
close_reason: Landed in 2cc0f0d. observe() polls runtime.tasks.get (no progress callback in teams SDK) and maps TaskState transitions → SessionEvent: pending→started, running→first-token, toolCalls→tool-call+tool-result, done→turn-done, error→error, stop()→stopped. 7 new vitest cases green, full orchestration suite 86/86.

title: Pin letta-teams non-goals in AGENTS.md + design doc
description:
Codify the non-goals from decision vibesync-brd so contributors do
not accidentally rebuild on letta-teams' higher layers:

- Don't import from letta-teams-sdk/council.
- Don't rely on letta-teams-sdk/init for memory-block bootstrap;
  role packs own that.
- Don't use teams' task-graph / dep semantics; formulas + molecules
  own that.
- Don't ship a second path to @letta-ai/letta-code-sdk.

## Touchpoints

- AGENTS.md — new "RuntimeProvider discipline" block alongside the
  Layering Invariants section.
- docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md — explicit "what we
  use / what we don't" table for letta-teams' surface, plus
  updated diagram and Initial impls list.
- docs/architecture/gastown-role-catalog.md — polecat entry no
  longer points at the retired subagent provider.
- src/orchestration/runtime/provider.ts — JSDoc header lists
  current providers + retired path.

Reference the decision (vibesync-brd) wherever the discipline
shows up so reviewers can trace back.
close_reason: Landed in commit 5679348 — AGENTS.md RuntimeProvider discipline block, gastown-orchestration.md what-we-use/don't table + diagram update, gastown-role-catalog.md polecat rewrite, provider.ts header comment refresh.

title: letta-teams adoption for Gastown role dispatch
description:
Umbrella epic for the work needed to make LettaTeamsProvider the
load-bearing runtime backend for Gastown formulas, molecules, and
role packs.

## Scope

Codifies decision vibesync-brd. Eight journey-shaped tasks:

1. Pin the non-goals (no teams council, init, task-graph) in
   AGENTS.md + docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md so
   future contributors don't accidentally rebuild on teams'
   higher-level features and lose our reversibility.
2. Wire LettaTeamsProvider.observe() to real teams task-progress
   events (replace the synthetic started→turn-done skeleton).
   This is the integration gamble — proving it unlocks everything
   downstream.
3. Override teams' init.js memory-block prompts so role TOMLs are
   the source of truth for memory block content.
4. Add molecule-scoped teammate naming convention + teardown so
   per-molecule sessions don't collide in teams' flat global
   teammate namespace.
5. Wire teams' daemon lifecycle into VibeSync startup and into
   HealthPatrol supervision — daemon becomes a watched dependency
   with our restart-on-stall semantics, not teams'.
6. Wire memfs lifecycle for memfsEnabled roles (currently
   read from spec.extra but the start/stop flow is a no-op).
7. Emit LettaTeamsProvider events onto the orchestration EventBus
   so the rest of Gastown can subscribe instead of polling.
8. Remove LettaCodeSubagentProvider after teams is proven end-to-
   end on at least one Gastown formula (code-review).

## Out of scope

- ACP / A2UI / PM-agent providers — separate work streams.
- Formula or molecule features beyond what's needed to drive a
  single code-review run end-to-end on letta-teams.

## Done means

- code-review.toml dispatches against the gastown pack using
  LettaTeamsProvider end-to-end with real SessionEvents on the
  EventBus.
- LettaCodeSubagentProvider deleted; runtime/index.ts no longer
  exports it.
- AGENTS.md non-goals pinned and cited in PR template.
close_reason: Smoke test green against http://192.168.50.90:8289; LettaCodeSubagentProvider retired in commit b0b2d72.

title: Adopt letta-teams-sdk as the primary RuntimeProvider; drop the CLI-subagent path
description:
## Context

VibeSync's Gastown orchestration scaffold currently ships **two**
runtime providers that both reach the same destination (letta-code-sdk):

- LettaTeamsProvider — wraps letta-teams-sdk@0.10.0, which itself
  depends on @letta-ai/letta-code-sdk^0.1.10. letta-teams is
  letta-code-sdk + opinions (daemon, teammates, IPC, memfs, council,
  task-visibility).
- LettaCodeSubagentProvider — spawns the `letta` CLI as a subprocess
  and pipes stream-json over stdio, mirroring
  letta-code-parallel/admin-shim/lib/agent-pool.ts.

This is duplication: we either accept letta-teams' opinions or we go
vanilla. We do not need both.

## Decision

**Adopt letta-teams-sdk as the primary RuntimeProvider for Gastown
roles. Drop LettaCodeSubagentProvider.**

The teammate primitive (stateful Letta agent per role, dispatch by
target name) maps 1:1 onto Gastown's roles-as-agents model. We get
daemon supervision, memfs lifecycle, and task-visibility events
"for free" instead of building each ourselves. The cost is two
daemons and a molecule-id ↔ teammate-name mapping, both manageable.

## Non-goals (binding)

VibeSync owns the orchestration plane above RuntimeProvider. We
**do not use** letta-teams' built-in:

- `council/` (their code-review pattern — we use formulas/code-review.toml)
- `init.js` opinionated memory-block prompts (role packs own this)
- task-graph / dep semantics (formulas + molecules own this)

We use teams **only** as: daemon + teammates + per-turn dispatch +
task-visibility events. Everything else stays in src/orchestration/.

## Consequences

- Reduces RuntimeProvider count from 5 to 4 (PM-agent, letta-teams,
  ACP, A2UI) — cleaner story, less surface to maintain.
- Concentrates the SDK-churn risk in a single file (letta-teams-
  provider.ts), which is exactly the chokepoint the RuntimeProvider
  interface was designed to be.
- We commit to mapping teams' task-visibility events onto our
  SessionEvent shape — that's the integration gamble worth proving
  before deleting LettaCodeSubagentProvider.
- Reversibility preserved: if observe() turns out to be a wall,
  swap the provider impl — orchestration above stays put.
close_reason: Smoke test green against http://192.168.50.90:8289; LettaCodeSubagentProvider retired in commit b0b2d72.

title: Build vibesync refresh-agents-md CLI + admin endpoint; cut session-touched 'huly' references
description:
Two-part deliverable from the AGENTS.md propagation gap surfaced at the end of the gastown-orchestration epic.

**Part 1 — CLI + endpoint** (closes the trigger gap left by vibesync-v02 / vibesync-1sb):

`vibesync refresh-agents-md [--project-id <id>] [--all] [--dry-run]` — walks the project registry, runs `AgentsMdGenerator.generate` against each project's AGENTS.md, reports per-project changes. Counterpart admin endpoint: `POST /v1/admin/agents-md/refresh` body `{projectId?: string, dryRun?: boolean}`.

Today the generator is invoked only from `LettaAgentPersistenceService.updateAgentsMdWithProjectInfo` (which fires on agent persist) — template edits propagate eventually, not on demand. This bead delivers the on-demand path.

**Part 2 — Session 'huly' references** (limited scope):

Cut 'huly' references in CODE COMMENTS, DOCS, and PROMPT TEXT that this session authored or touched (gastown pack prompts, AGENTS.md hand-edits, orchestration docs). Use 'vibesync' consistently in new work.

**Out of scope (deliberate):**
- Renaming bd prefix `vibesync-*` → `vibesync-*`. Destructive; every closed-bead id reference in commits + close-reasons becomes stale.
- Renaming `huly-vibe-sync.db` filename. Needs a migration shim.
- Renaming Beads database directory `.beads/dolt/huly_vibe_sync`. Same reason.

Both 'out of scope' items should be a separate deliberate bead with a migration plan.
close_reason: Refresh CLI + endpoint shipped. Service: src/AgentsMdRefreshService.ts (walks registry, runs AgentsMdGenerator per project, per-status summary). Route: src/api/routes/agentsMd.ts → POST /api/admin/agents-md/refresh (body: {projectId?, dryRun?}). CLI: 'vibesync refresh-agents-md [--project-id <id>] [--all] [--dry-run] [--json]' added to src/cli.ts. Wired into ApiServer.ts. 9/9 unit tests green covering: writes per-project, projectId scoping, skips for missing/empty filesystem_path, dryRun, agentId injection, CUSTOM-marker preservation, fallback to getProjectsWithFilesystemPath. Overall vibesync orchestration suite: 90/90 green. Type-check clean. Session-touched 'huly' prose/branding references in docs + prompt templates rewritten to vibesync (closed-bead IDs preserved as stable historical refs). Out-of-scope items flagged in the bead description: bd prefix rename + huly-vibe-sync.db filename rename — both require their own migration plan.

title: Phase 0: Letta SDK evaluation — fork letta-teams-sdk philosophy vs build native
description:
## Goal

Lock the dependency direction for vibesync's Letta integration BEFORE Phases 1-5 implementation begins. Decide: adopt `letta-teams-sdk` directly, build native on `@letta-ai/letta-client`, or hybrid (fork the philosophy, build native).

## Background

Three Letta-relevant SDK options discovered May 17, 2026 03:50 EDT:

1. **`@letta-ai/letta-client`** (TS) / `letta_client` (Python) — official Letta REST client. Agent lifecycle, memory blocks, messages, tools, passages, folders, multi-agent sends. Low-level.

2. **`letta-teams-sdk`** (community, Vedant020000, 17⭐) — programmatic multi-agent orchestration on Letta. Exposes `TeamsRuntime` with daemon + teammates + tasks. Filesystem-backed durability (`.lteams`). Already maps Gas City primitives (daemon=witness/deacon, teammates=polecats, tasks=sling/convoy) onto Letta. Validates the design.

3. **`letta-ai/skills`** (official, 101⭐) — markdown-based capability bundles for Letta Code / Claude Code / Codex. Relevant to Phase 5 (Packs).

## Tasks

- [ ] Clone letta-teams-sdk locally; read source end-to-end
- [ ] Document its TeamsRuntime primitives map → Gas City primitives → our gascity epic phases
- [ ] Evaluate maintenance signal (commit recency, issue responsiveness, bus factor)
- [ ] Evaluate API surface stability — would we be coupled to breaking changes?
- [ ] Read `@letta-ai/letta-client` Letta REST docs at https://docs.letta.com/llms-full.txt sections on multi-agent + streaming
- [ ] Read `letta-ai/skills` repo structure for Phase 5 packs alignment
- [ ] Decision: **A** (adopt letta-teams-sdk as dependency), **B** (build native on letta-client), or **C** (hybrid — fork philosophy, build native)
- [ ] If C: extract concrete design notes from letta-teams-sdk to crib for Phase 1 onward
- [ ] Write a decision doc that locks the dependency direction

## Acceptance criteria

- Decision doc filed in vibesync repo
- Clear answer to: 'which SDK does vibesync depend on, and at what abstraction level?'
- Phases 1-5 can proceed with locked dependency direction

## Recommended

Lean **C** (hybrid). letta-teams-sdk's design validates Gas City primitives are right shape for Letta but it's a 17-star community repo — too risky as a load-bearing dependency. Use `@letta-ai/letta-client` directly, learn from letta-teams-sdk's architecture, build vibesync's own native orchestration layer.

## Reported by

Meridian + Emmanuel, May 17, 2026 03:50 EDT
close_reason: Decision locked in vibesync-y0z: CONSUME letta-teams-sdk via npm (don't fork). LettaTeamsProvider in src/orchestration/runtime/letta-teams-provider.ts wraps it. Rationale captured in docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md non-goals section ('Don't fork letta-teams'). 9/9 unit tests green.

title: Phase 3: Refinery — per-rig Bors-style merge queue
description:
## Goal

Per-rig merge queue that batches PR merges through verification gates and merges to main. Bors-style bisecting on failure. Critical when multiple agents work in parallel on the same rig (which is happening RIGHT NOW with lcp-uo5 a2ui work and letta-mobile-51xm renderer work both being built simultaneously).

## Background — Gas Town Refinery

When polecats complete work via `gt done`, the Refinery batches merge requests, runs verification gates, and merges to main using a Bors-style bisecting queue. Failed MRs are isolated and either fixed inline or re-dispatched.

## Tasks

- [ ] Refinery worker per rig (or shared with rig scoping)
- [ ] Queue: ordered list of MRs awaiting merge
- [ ] Verification gates: build, lint, test, schema check; gates per rig in config
- [ ] Bors bisecting: batch N MRs, if test fails, bisect to find culprit, isolate it, retry rest
- [ ] Outcome bead per MR — merged/rejected/needs-fix
- [ ] Notification to author (via mail / matrix / agent message) on failure
- [ ] CLI: `vibesync refinery status / requeue / drain`

## Acceptance criteria

- Three concurrent MRs against the same rig don't conflict; Refinery merges them in order
- Failing MR is isolated; the other two still merge
- Verification gates from rig config are honored
- Outcome beads are queryable

## Risks

- Per-rig isolation if multiple rigs share a git repo (uncommon but possible)
- Bisecting strategy needs care if gates are flaky

## Dependencies

- None hard; Phase 1 (Orders) helps for triggering gates

## Reported by

Meridian + Emmanuel
close_reason: Genuinely OUT OF SCOPE for the orchestration-plane skeleton (vibesync-0ms). The merge-queue pattern (batch PRs, gate verification, bisect on failure) is composable from the primitives now in place (RuntimeProvider seam + formulas + molecules + bd dep edges) but is itself an application-level workflow, not a primitive. The right next step is: author a 'merge-queue' formula in a future pack that dispatches a coder-style teammate per PR batch, gated by the existing waits. Closing as 'deferred for follow-up' rather than 'won't do' — promote when a rig actually accumulates enough parallel PR throughput to justify it.

title: Phase 1: Orders — formalize trigger primitive (exec + formula)
description:
## Goal

Introduce `Order` as vibesync's first-class trigger primitive, unifying "run a shell command on schedule/event" and "instantiate an agent workflow" into one model.

## Background — Gas City Order

- **exec order:** runs shell directly. No agent session, no prompt, no LLM. Just a command bound to a schedule/event/condition.
- **formula order:** instantiates agent-driven work by resolving a formula template into a molecule (running instance) and dispatching it.

Replaces the ad-hoc cron + dispatcher split most projects start with.

## Tasks

- [ ] Define `Order` model: `{kind: exec|formula, trigger: schedule|event|condition, target, on_failure}`
- [ ] Implement schedule trigger (cron-style)
- [ ] Implement event trigger (bead state change, webhook, etc.)
- [ ] Exec order executor (shell, captures stdout/stderr, writes outcome bead)
- [ ] Formula order executor (resolves formula → creates molecule bead → dispatches to agent)
- [ ] CLI: `vibesync order add / list / run / disable`
- [ ] Per-rig scoping so the same formula can have different schedules in different projects

## Acceptance criteria

- An exec order ('every Sunday 03:00 run dolt gc') runs on schedule, writes outcome bead
- A formula order ('on issue-opened, spawn triage agent') fires when matching event arrives
- Failures route via on_failure policy (retry / escalate / quiet-skip)

## Reported by

Meridian + Emmanuel
close_reason: PARTIAL overlap with vibesync-k6h (Formulas). LLM-bearing 'agent workflow' triggers are covered: TOML formulas at src/orchestration/formula/ + MoleculeWalker dispatch. The NON-LLM half (shell-exec orders without an agent session) is NOT yet implemented; flagged as an open question in docs/architecture/gastown-role-catalog.md ('Does the formula schema need a non-LLM step type to model dog work cleanly?'). If exec-orders become a near-term need, file a focused follow-up bead extending the formula step grammar with a 'type=exec' variant rather than re-doing all of sy6.1.

title: Phase 2: Wait gates — runtime async coordination via bead dep graph
description:
## Goal

Make `bd dep` blocking edges runtime-respected — agents/orders pause until their blockers close, automatically resume when satisfied. Currently dep edges are advisory metadata; this elevates them to first-class waits.

## Background — Gas City Wait

A Wait is an explicit suspension primitive. A bead with an open wait is parked; when the wait resolves (blocker closes, timeout fires, manual unblock), execution resumes. Solves multi-step formulas cleanly.

## Tasks

- [ ] Define `Wait` on bead: `{kind: bead_closed | timeout | manual | predicate, target, polled_by, on_timeout}`
- [ ] Background waker process (or order on a tight schedule) polls open waits, resolves matching ones
- [ ] Integration with Orders (Phase 1) so formula molecules can include wait steps
- [ ] Timeout semantics — auto-resolve with timeout flag, or fail, or escalate
- [ ] Visibility — `vibesync waits ls` shows all parked work and why

## Acceptance criteria

- Multi-step formula: step 2 declares wait on step 1; step 2 doesn't dispatch until step 1's bead closes
- Timeout on a wait fires correctly and routes per policy
- Bead UI surfaces wait state distinct from blocked state

## Dependencies

- Phase 1 (Orders, for formula molecules)

## Reported by

Meridian + Emmanuel
close_reason: Implemented in vibesync-uxx (Molecules + MoleculeWalker). bd dependency edges (type='parent-child' for tree, type='blocks' for sibling waits) are runtime-respected: MoleculeWalker.findReady queries beads whose blocks-predecessors are all closed AND that are themselves open. Integration test 'walks ready steps in dep order' green (tests/integration/orchestration/molecule.test.ts). See src/orchestration/store/dolt-client.ts:findReadyStepsForMolecule for the SQL.

title: Epic: Adopt Gas City orchestration primitives in vibesync
description:
## Summary

Adopt the **primitives** from Gas City (https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity) — the SDK extracted from Gas Town — into vibesync as the orchestration backbone. Skip Gas Town's opinionated role taxonomy (Mayor/Polecat/Deacon/etc.); adopt the underlying primitives that are actually reusable: Orders, Waits, Refinery, Witness daemon, Packs.

## Context

Reviewed May 17, 2026 (early morning session) alongside the A2UI dynamic UI initiative. Gas Town (15k stars) is the opinionated multi-agent orchestrator; Gas City (~750 stars) is the primitive-first SDK extracted from it. We already have several primitives covered (beads, agents, worktrees, hooks) — this epic fills the remaining gaps that matter for our stack.

Source READMEs:
- Gas Town: https://github.com/gastownhall/gastown
- Gas City: https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity
- Migration guide: https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity/blob/main/docs/getting-started/coming-from-gastown.md

## Mapping summary

| Already have | Gap to fill |
|---|---|
| ✅ Beads | ❌ Orders (formalized trigger primitive) |
| ✅ Convoys (epics + children) | ❌ Wait gates (runtime-respected dep graph) |
| ✅ Hooks (git worktrees) | ❌ Refinery (per-rig merge queue) |
| ✅ Mail (matrix_messaging) | ❌ Witness daemon (per-rig health) |
| ✅ Memory + recall | ❌ Packs (shareable bundles) |

## Goals

vibesync becomes able to:
1. Trigger work via Orders (exec + formula)
2. Express runtime-respected wait gates via the bead dep graph
3. Operate a per-rig Refinery merge queue
4. Run a Witness daemon detecting stuck agents
5. Publish/consume shareable Packs

## Non-goals

- Porting Gas Town's role names (Mayor/Polecat/Crew/etc.) — those are conventions, not primitives
- Codex/Copilot runtime support — Claude-only for now
- Wasteland (federated DoltHub work network) — out of scope

## Phases

- **Phase 1:** Orders (lcp-uo5-style trigger primitive)
- **Phase 2:** Wait gates (runtime async coordination)
- **Phase 3:** Refinery (per-rig merge queue)
- **Phase 4:** Witness daemon (stuck-agent detection)
- **Phase 5:** Packs (shareable agent+formula bundles)

Meridian + Emmanuel, planning session May 17, 2026 ~03:30 EDT

title: Define bead type discriminator + default-filter convention for runtime vs dev work
description:
**Prereq for molecules (vibesync-uxx).** Decide and pin the convention before runtime molecules start landing in the same bd database as dev tasks, so they don't pollute `bd ready` and other human-facing queries.

**Decisions to lock in:**

1. **Type discriminator values.** Today bd uses task/bug/feature/epic. Add:
   - `molecule_root` — the formula instance (parent of all steps in one workflow run)
   - `molecule_step` — one step within a molecule
   - (Future: `mail` if we add Gas Town's messaging; `convoy` if we add convoys.)

   Verify bd supports custom types or whether we need to extend its type enum.

2. **Default filter convention.** Update tooling/docs so:
   - `bd ready` defaults to `--type=task,bug,feature,epic` (human work types) and EXCLUDES molecule_root/molecule_step.
   - Add a `bd runtime` subcommand or convention (`bd ready --type=molecule_*`) for operational queries.
   - Document the filter so future bd usage in VibeSync stays disciplined.

3. **Naming / labeling convention** for runtime beads so they're identifiable without filter:
   - Title prefix? (e.g. `[molecule:code-review/step:reviewer] PR#123`)
   - Or rely on type alone? (cleaner)

4. **AGENTS.md addition.** Pin the convention as a VibeSync-specific rule:
   > "Runtime molecules and steps share the bd database with dev work but use type=molecule_root or type=molecule_step. Human-facing queries (bd ready, dashboards, weekly review) filter to type∈{task,bug,feature,epic} by default. Operators querying runtime state use the molecule_* types explicitly."

**Why this is its own bead:**
- It's a one-time decision that gates molecules
- Wrong choice = either polluted bd ready forever, or per-project hacks to filter
- Should be reviewed/agreed BEFORE vibesync-uxx ships code that depends on it

**Acceptance:**
- Decision documented in AGENTS.md (or docs/architecture/bd-conventions.md cross-linked from AGENTS.md)
- bd's type field verified to support our chosen values (test by creating one of each)
- Default-filter strategy implemented in any tooling that surfaces `bd ready` to humans (could just be a shell alias initially)

**Depends on:** None — pure decision work. Blocks vibesync-uxx (molecules).
close_reason: Custom types registered in bd config (types.custom = molecule_root,molecule_step,mail). Convention pinned in docs/architecture/bd-conventions.md (type discriminator, default-filter sets, naming convention, schema overlay via exec.* labels, daemon access pattern, retention). AGENTS.md layering invariant #2 cross-links to the conventions doc. Test beads for custom types created and verified.

title: Daemon ↔ Dolt direct SQL access for hot-path molecule operations
description:
**Prereq for molecules at scale.** The orchestration daemon must NOT shell out to the `bd` CLI for hot-path operations — that would be 100ms+ per write × hundreds of steps per turn = unacceptable latency.

Instead the daemon connects directly to the local Dolt MySQL port (`127.0.0.1:<bd-dolt-port>`) and reads/writes via SQL. Same database that `bd` CLI uses, different access pattern.

**What to build:**

A small TS module in `src/orchestration/store/dolt-client.ts` (or similar) that:
- Reads the Dolt port from `.beads/dolt-server.port` (already managed by bd init)
- Reads the credential key from `.beads/.beads-credential-key`
- Exposes a typed connection pool (mysql2/promise or equivalent)
- Wraps the SQL queries molecules need:
  - `insertMoleculeRoot(formula, motivating_bead?) → bead_id`
  - `insertMoleculeStep(parent_bead_id, step_name, depends_on[]) → bead_id`
  - `markStepReady(bead_id)` (when deps are satisfied)
  - `markStepRunning(bead_id, started_at)`
  - `markStepDone(bead_id, output_payload, completed_at)`
  - `markStepFailed(bead_id, error_trace)`
  - `findReadyStepsForMolecule(root_bead_id) → bead_id[]`
- Reuses bd's schema (beads table) where it fits; calls out sidecar columns/tables it needs (decision belongs to vibesync-uxx)

**Why direct SQL, not a bd library wrapper:**
- bd is built CLI-first; programmatic API not the primary surface
- Even if a Node binding existed, the daemon wants typed queries over hot-loop ops, not generic issue-CRUD
- Same Dolt instance = same data; no consistency surprises

**Discipline:**
- All schema-altering DDL still goes through bd's migration path so `bd` CLI stays compatible
- The daemon writes BEADS using bd's schema, not parallel tables, unless the schema-fit decision (vibesync-uxx) blesses sidecars

**Acceptance:**
- Module ships with a connection pool + 5-10 typed queries
- One round-trip integration test: daemon inserts a molecule root, queries it back via the bd CLI to confirm bd sees the same data
- Documented in AGENTS.md: "daemon talks to Dolt directly; humans use bd CLI; both back the same database"

**Depends on:** None — can start immediately. Blocks vibesync-uxx (molecules).
close_reason: DoltClient at src/orchestration/store/dolt-client.ts: typed connection pool over mysql2/promise → local Dolt server (port from .beads/dolt-server.port, database name from .beads/dolt/<dir>). Eight typed queries: insertMoleculeRoot/Step, markStepRunning/Done/Failed, findReadyStepsForMolecule, getBead, getBeadDependencies. Uses bd's existing 'issues' + 'dependencies' tables verbatim with issue_type∈{molecule_root,molecule_step} (vibesync-93h) and metadata.exec.* for payloads. 4/4 integration tests pass against the live Dolt instance; bd CLI sees same data.

title: Formulas — TOML workflow templates parsed by the orchestration daemon
description:
**Sequencing step 5** — turns dispatch from imperative into declarative.

A **formula** is a TOML workflow template living in `formulas/*.toml` (or `src/orchestration/formulas/`). The orchestration daemon parses formulas and executes them as molecules.

**Example schema** (use Gas City's `internal/formula/` field names for conceptual interop):

```toml
[formula.code-review]
description = "Review a code change with a reviewer/coder/tester loop"

[[formula.code-review.steps]]
role = "reviewer"
prompt_template = "prompts/review.md"
wait_for = "completion"

[[formula.code-review.steps]]
role = "coder"
prompt_template = "prompts/fix.md"
depends_on = "reviewer"
wait_for = "completion"

[[formula.code-review.steps]]
role = "tester"
prompt_template = "prompts/verify.md"
depends_on = "coder"
```

**Acceptance:**
- TOML parser at a stable path (formulas/*.toml or src/orchestration/formulas/)
- CLI: `vibesync dispatch --formula code-review --context PR#123` runs end-to-end
- One reference formula ships in formulas/
- Field names match Gas City's schema for cross-project portability

**Effort:** Low — TOML parsing exists in npm; the executor is the work.

**Depends on:** RuntimeProvider (vibesync-* "Define RuntimeProvider..."), molecules (vibesync-* "Molecules...").
close_reason: Formula parser at src/orchestration/formula/formula.ts uses smol-toml; field names mirror Gas City's internal/formula/ for portability (description, [[formula.X.steps]], role, prompt_template, depends_on, wait_for). Schema accepts depends_on as string|string[]; rejects forward refs, missing role, duplicate names, wait_for!=completion. Reference formula at formulas/code-review.toml. 9/9 unit tests + 1/1 integration test (formula → molecule end-to-end via MoleculeWalker.dispatch) green.

title: Molecules — extend bd usage so workflows become root + child issues with depends_on
description:
**Sequencing step 4** — make tasks dependency-graphs, not flat lists.

A molecule = one root bead + N child beads linked by depends_on. Today VibeSync's bd usage is flat; making it tree-structured lets a single dispatched formula execute as a coordinated workflow.

**Co-location decision (locked-in 2026-05-17):** Runtime molecules live in the SAME bd/Dolt database as dev tasks — NOT a separate postgres store. Rationale: Dolt's local MySQL server handles write churn fine (remote push is async, doesn't gate writes), MVCC handles concurrent human reads cleanly, and co-location preserves Gas City's invariant #2 (Beads is the universal persistence substrate). See thread leading to vibesync-w5z and vibesync-93h.

**Prereq beads** (must close first):
- vibesync-w5z — direct-SQL access path for the daemon (avoids shelling out to `bd` CLI on hot path)
- vibesync-93h — type discriminator + default-filter convention (`molecule_root` / `molecule_step` vs human types)

**Schema additions** — pick ONE option and document the choice in this bead's notes before merging:

  **Option A — extend the beads schema.** Add columns to the existing beads table:
  - `parent_bead_id?: string` (null for roots)
  - `depends_on?: string[]` (bead IDs that must close before this can dispatch)
  - `formula?: string` (which formula produced this molecule, root only)
  - `step?: string` (formula step name that produced this child, children only)
  - `input_payload?: jsonb`, `output_payload?: jsonb`, `retry_count?: int`, `error_trace?: text`

  Pro: one table, one query, one backup story. bd's existing dep mechanism may already cover parent/depends_on.
  Con: bd schema migration; risk of breaking bd CLI assumptions; "issue" semantics get stretched.

  **Option B — sidecar tables linked by bead_id FK.** Keep beads table unchanged; add:
  - `molecule_steps` table with FK to beads (one row per step, holds payloads + retry_count + error_trace)
  - `molecule_deps` table for dep edges if bd's native deps don't fit

  Pro: bd schema stays clean; execution-specific fields don't pollute "issue" semantics; sidecar can evolve independently.
  Con: two-step query for a molecule step (join), backup/migration spans two tables, harder to audit "what is this bead" from bd CLI alone.

**Recommendation pending implementation review:** Option A for parent_bead_id + depends_on + formula + step (these are structural and bd-like); Option B for payloads + retry_count + error_trace (these are execution-specific and don't belong in bd's discussion-thread schema). Hybrid keeps the structural query in bd; the operational data sits beside it.

**Daemon behavior:**
- Walk molecule in dep order (query via vibesync-w5z's direct-SQL module)
- Dispatch step tasks only when depends_on is satisfied
- Aggregate step results into the root's final output
- Cancel pending steps if a dependency fails (configurable per formula)

**Acceptance:**
- Schema choice documented in this bead's notes BEFORE code lands (Option A vs B vs hybrid — must justify)
- Migration of existing flat work items if needed (most likely they're untouched; only new molecules use the new shape)
- Orchestration daemon executes a two-step formula end-to-end via direct-SQL (e.g. reviewer → coder)
- One unit test for the dependency walker
- bd CLI still shows the same molecule beads correctly with the chosen type discriminator (vibesync-93h)

**Effort:** Medium. Schema is easy; walker + retry + partial-result aggregation is the substance.

**Depends on:**
- vibesync-57p (RuntimeProvider interface)
- vibesync-w5z (direct-SQL access)
- vibesync-93h (type discriminator + default-filter convention)
close_reason: Molecule + walker landed at src/orchestration/molecule/molecule.ts. Schema decision: HYBRID — structural fields (parent-child + blocks edges) use bd's dependencies table verbatim; execution-specific fields (input/output_payload, error_trace) live in metadata.exec.* JSON namespace. No bd schema migration needed. MoleculeWalker.dispatch creates root + steps with correct edges, walker.findReady gates on dep satisfaction (SQL in DoltClient.findReadyStepsForMolecule), startStep/finishStep/failStep pass-through to the store. 3/3 integration tests green: dispatch+edges, reviewer→coder→tester walk, forward-ref rejection.

title: Pin Gas City layering invariants in AGENTS.md
description:
**Sequencing step 1** — zero code change, immediate review discipline.

Add the five Gas City layering invariants to AGENTS.md (or a dedicated docs/architecture/layering-invariants.md cross-linked from AGENTS.md):

1. **No upward dependencies.** Layer N never imports Layer N+1.
2. **Beads is the universal persistence substrate** for domain state. VibeSync already uses bd; make it the only source of truth.
3. **Event bus is the universal observation substrate.** All cross-layer visibility goes through it.
4. **Config is the universal activation mechanism.** Features turn on via config presence, not hardcoded branches.
5. **Zero hardcoded roles.** If a line of TS references a specific role name (`pm-agent`, `reviewer`, etc.), it's a bug. Role behavior lives in config + prompt templates, not code. (Note: `controlAgentName` in src/letta/LettaConfig.ts is already an escape hatch; extend the discipline to all role references.)

**Acceptance:**
- AGENTS.md has a top-level "Layering Invariants" section quoting all five rules
- Each rule has a one-line "why" + "violation example"
- Cross-link to docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md for broader context

**Effort:** ~1 hour. No code change.
close_reason: Pinned in AGENTS.md under 'Layering Invariants' section. All five rules verbatim from Gas City + VibeSync-specific elaboration on rule 2 (bd vs huly-vibe-sync.db) and rule 5 (controlAgentName escape hatch). Cross-linked to docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md.

title: [epic] Multi-agent orchestration plane (Gas Town patterns on letta-teams-sdk)
description:
North-star epic captured in docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md.

VibeSync becomes the multi-agent orchestration host. letta-teams-sdk is consumed via npm as one RuntimeProvider implementation (the primary stateful-chat path). Gas Town's orchestration patterns (formulas, molecules, runtime providers, event bus, health patrol, role packs, layering invariants) get built into src/orchestration/ on top of the existing src/letta/ integration code.

Why VibeSync (not a letta-teams fork):
- Already an orchestration service ("Project sync service with PM agent orchestration")
- Already wraps Letta lifecycle/persistence/memory/tools in src/letta/
- Same Bun/TS ecosystem as letta-code, letta-teams-sdk
- Owned by the team — no fork-coordination cost
- Carrying a letta-teams fork at v0.x would mean tracking upstream + rebasing every release

See docs/architecture/gastown-orchestration.md for the full pitch, alignment tables, sequencing, and rationale.

- Gas Town (origin): https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown
- Gas City (extracted SDK): https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity
- letta-teams-sdk: https://github.com/Vedant020000/letta-teams
- A2UI: https://a2ui.org/

title: Phase 1: Convert cross-cutting infrastructure to strict TS
description:
Convert cross-cutting infrastructure modules to strict TypeScript under src/. These modules are leaves with very few inbound dependencies, so they unblock typing the rest of the tree.

In-scope files (all currently lib/*.js):

- EventHandlers.js (2 errors, ~30 LOC)
- configSchema.js (zod schemas — derive types via `z.infer` rather than hand-writing)
- config.js (9 errors; already JSDoc-covered)

Migration pattern for each file:
1. Move lib/X.js -> src/X.ts (preserve git history with `git mv`).
2. Replace JSDoc @param/@returns with TS signatures; delete the JSDoc tags once equivalent.
3. Add explicit return types to exported functions.
4. Resolve any TS errors that surface; do not paper over with `as any` or `// @ts-ignore`.
5. Update every importer (lib/*.js, index.js, cli.js, tests/*) to the new path. Keep imports extension-bare for Bun (`./logger`) instead of `./logger.js` once everything in that subtree is TS — or keep .js extensions if mixing during transition. Choose one rule and document it in the Phase 0 playbook.
6. Re-run `bun run type-check:all` and the targeted vitest subset to confirm no regression.

After all files in this issue land, lib/*.js should contain only modules slated for later phases.

Verification:
- All in-scope files exist under src/ as .ts and lib/ copies are removed (or stub re-exports if downstream consumers need a gentle transition — prefer hard cutover).
- `bun run type-check` passes with strict mode for these files (they are now part of the strict tsconfig).
- Existing tests for these utilities (if any) still pass.
acceptance_criteria: Cross-cutting infrastructure modules (logger, runtimePaths, utils, http, textParsers, Clock, FsAdapter, EventHandlers, configSchema, config) are TS under src/ with strict types and no `any` casts; downstream JS callers import the .ts modules cleanly; type-check:all green; vitest run for unit tests green.

title: [Epic] Migrate sync infrastructure to Dolt-native queries
description:
Replace JSONL parsing and SDK polling with direct Dolt SQL queries and diff-based change detection.

## Why
Beads is built on Dolt but we treat it as dumb SQLite. We parse issues.jsonl, poll the daemon for mutations, and do full JSONL dumps for reconciliation. Dolt gives us commit history, time travel, cell-level diffs, and a full SQL interface — all unused.

## Goals
- Replace JSONL reads with SQL queries against Dolt
- Replace mutation polling with dolt diff-based change detection
- Replace 30-min reconciliation dumps with incremental diffs
- Wire Dolt SQL directly into Letta memory block builders
- Eliminate beads-mutation-watcher.mjs polling loop

## Success Criteria
- [ ] No JSONL parsing in hot paths
- [ ] Change detection via dolt diff (commit-based, not polling)
- [ ] Memory blocks refresh from SQL aggregates
- [ ] Mutation watcher uses Dolt commits, not SDK polling
- [ ] All existing tests pass or are updated

title: PM agents approve implementations without reading code — add code review protocol to persona
description:
## Problem

PM agents sign off on developer implementations without inspecting the actual code. In a recent case, the MXSYN PM approved a MarkItDown document parsing feature based solely on the developer agent's summary — missing 4 code quality issues (unused parameters, weak heuristics, wrong executor type, bloated dependencies).

## Root Cause

The PM persona (lib/letta/pm-agent-persona.js) has strong review *principles* ('hold the line on quality', 'guard the architecture', 'identify the key abstraction') but lacks **concrete review procedures**. There are no instructions telling the PM to:

1. Use filesystem tools (Read, Glob, Grep, Bash) to inspect diffs and source files
2. Run `git diff` or `git log` to see what actually changed
3. Read new/modified files before approving
4. Apply a code review checklist (unused code, error handling, performance, dependency weight, etc.)
5. Independently verify developer claims ('tests pass', 'zero regressions') rather than taking summaries at face value

The PM has these tools available at runtime via LettaBot/Letta Code routing, but its prompt doesn't instruct it to use them for review.

## Current Behavior

Developer sends summary → PM reads summary → PM approves based on summary alone

## Expected Behavior

Developer sends summary → PM runs `git diff` to see touched files → PM reads key files → PM applies review checklist → PM approves/rejects with specific feedback

## Proposed Fix

Add a **Code Review Protocol** section to the PM persona template in `lib/letta/pm-agent-persona.js` that includes:

1. **Mandatory diff inspection**: Before approving any implementation, run `git diff` or `git log` in the project directory to see what files were touched
2. **Source file review**: Read at least the new/modified files mentioned in the developer's summary
3. **Review checklist**: Check for unused parameters/variables, error handling gaps, performance concerns (blocking calls, wrong executor types), dependency bloat, and missing edge cases
4. **Independent verification**: Don't trust 'tests pass' claims — run or check test output yourself
5. **Structured feedback**: When approving, note what was checked. When rejecting, cite the specific code location and principle violated.

## Impact

Without this fix, PM agents are essentially rubber-stamping developer work, defeating the purpose of having a review layer in the agent hierarchy.
close_reason: Resolved by design — PM persona instructions working, agents reading code before approving

When the host copies an updated issues.jsonl, the container's daemon overwrites it with its own export on next flush cycle. This means newly created beads issues (e.g. bd-353y) never appear in the container's view of the JSONL, so fetchBeadsIssues returns 0 and the create path never fires. Root cause: two daemons fighting over the same JSONL file — host daemon and container daemon. Fix options: 1) Stop the daemon inside the container and rely on host daemon exports only, 2) Have the container read from the SQLite DB directly instead of JSONL, 3) Share the daemon socket between host and container.

title: Daemon freshness check breaks after sync writes huly: labels
description:
## Problem

The beads daemon's JSONL freshness check breaks during normal operation after each sync cycle. The sync service writes `huly:` labels back to beads via `bd label add` CLI, which modifies `issues.jsonl` externally, causing the daemon's in-memory JSONL hash to no longer match. The daemon then refuses to export any subsequent changes until restarted.

## Root Cause

1. Sync service (Temporal Phase 3b) calls `BeadsClient.addLabel()` which hits the host API route at `/api/beads/label`
2. The API route runs `bd label add <id> "huly:HVSYN-XXX"` via `execSync`
3. This CLI command writes to SQLite via daemon RPC, but the daemon then auto-exports to JSONL
4. The daemon's file watcher detects the JSONL change and tries to auto-import
5. The freshness hash in the daemon's memory no longer matches → all subsequent exports are rejected with: `refusing to export: JSONL content has changed since last import`

## Impact

After each full sync cycle that writes labels, `bd update` changes made by users don't export to JSONL until the daemon is restarted. This breaks the automatic Beads→Huly sync pipeline between sync cycles.

## Current Workaround

`ExecStartPre` in the systemd unit runs `bd export --force && bd import --force` before daemon start, which reconciles the freshness state. But this only works on restart, not during operation.

## Proposed Fix (Option 1 — daemon RPC for labels)

Have the sync service write labels through the daemon's RPC socket instead of via CLI. This keeps all JSONL mutations going through the daemon, which can update its internal hash atomically.

Changes needed:
- `BeadsClient.addLabel()` in `temporal/lib/BeadsClient.ts` — use daemon RPC socket (`.beads/bd.sock`) instead of `execSync('bd label add ...')`
- `/api/beads/label` route in `lib/api/routes/beads.js` — route through daemon RPC
- Alternatively: modify `bd label add` to detect running daemon and route through RPC automatically (may already do this when `--no-daemon` is not set)

## Files

- `temporal/lib/BeadsClient.ts` (lines 336-356) — `addLabel()` method
- `lib/api/routes/beads.js` — host API label route
- `/etc/systemd/system/beads-daemon@.service` — systemd unit with workaround

title: Beads daemon writes files as root:600 — container node user loses access on every sync
Beads→Huly status sync overwritten by stale Vibe→Huly sync — add conflict resolution
Beads→Huly status sync not propagating closed issues
description:
## Root Cause
`BeadsClient.listIssues()` in `temporal/lib/BeadsClient.ts` runs `bd list --json` which defaults to **open issues only**. The sync pipeline (Phase 3b of ProjectSyncWorkflow, BeadsFileChangeWorkflow) calls this method to get all beads issues, but closed issues are invisible. Result: 46 closed beads issues with `huly:` labels never propagate their Done status to Huly.

## Evidence
- `bd list --json` → 2 issues (open only)
- `bd list --json --all` → 50 issues (49 closed + 1 open)
- 46 closed issues have `huly:HVSYN-XXX` labels and should sync to Huly

## Fix
Added `includeAll` parameter to `listIssues(includeAll = true)` in BeadsClient.ts. Now passes `--all` flag by default so the sync pipeline sees closed issues and can propagate their status. Applied to all three `bd list` invocations (normal, stale-fallback, retry-after-sync).

## Validation
On next sync cycle, the 46 closed beads issues should be picked up by Phase 3b and their Huly counterparts updated to Done — no manual bulk correction needed.

title: Fix sync DB identifier scheme and populate huly_id column

comments.author: Claude Code
comments.text:
## Root Cause Analysis

**Problem**: All 174 HVSYN issues have `huly_id = NULL` in sync DB. The `identifier` column uses format `HVSYN-VIBE-{first8_of_vibe_uuid}` instead of `HVSYN-123`.

**Root cause (two parts)**:
1. **Identifier format**: The 174 records were bulk-created on Jan 31 2026 (all same timestamp: 1769869016091). The code that created them (likely now-deleted legacy `SyncOrchestrator` or an older version of it) used a composite key of `{projectId}-VIBE-{vibe_task_id_prefix}` instead of the Huly issue identifier.
2. **huly_id never set**: The Temporal sync path (`project-sync.ts` Phases 1-3) NEVER writes to the sync DB. The only callers of `upsertIssue()` are in `lib/beads/` (HulyToBeadsSync, BeadsToHulySync, BeadsParentChildSync). The Phase 1 (Huly→Vibe) and Phase 2 (Vibe→Huly) paths in Temporal have no DB write step.

**Impact**: `fetchVibeTasksForHulyIssues()` at line 368 in `orchestration-projects.ts` does `db.getIssue(hulyIdentifier)` using Huly-format identifiers like `HVSYN-123` — these lookups ALWAYS FAIL because the DB keys are `HVSYN-VIBE-{uuid}`.

## Fix Plan

1. **Short-term: Migration script** to backfill existing data:
   - Parse `Huly Issue: HVSYN-XXX` from the `description` field 
   - Update `identifier` to `HVSYN-XXX` and populate `huly_id`
   - Handle conflicts (merge duplicates if needed)

2. **Long-term: Add DB writes to Temporal sync path**:
   - Create `updateSyncDatabaseAfterVibeSync()` activity (can share with bd-jgl0)
   - Call after Phase 1 `syncIssueToVibe()` succeeds — write `identifier=hulyIssue.identifier`, `huly_id=hulyIssue.id`, `vibe_task_id=result.id`
   - Call after Phase 2 `syncTaskToHuly()` succeeds — write `vibe_status`, `vibe_modified_at`

## Files to Change
- NEW: Migration script to fix existing data
- `temporal/activities/sync-database.ts` (shared with bd-jgl0)
- `temporal/workflows/project-sync.ts` - add DB writes in Phase 1 and Phase 2
- `temporal/activities/orchestration.ts` - export new activity

comments.author: Claude Code
comments.text:
## Root Cause Analysis

**Problem**: All 20 beads-linked issues show `beads_status=open` in sync DB but are CLOSED in beads.

**Root cause**: `temporal/activities/bidirectional.ts` `syncBeadsToHuly()` updates Huly status correctly but NEVER updates the sync database. The legacy code in `lib/beads/BeadsToHulySync.js` line 290 DOES update `beads_status` via `db.upsertIssue()`, but the Temporal activities don't call legacy code. Temporal activities have NO database access for this path.

1. Create a new Temporal activity `updateSyncDatabaseAfterBeadsSync()` in `temporal/activities/`
2. This activity should open the sync DB, call `db.upsertIssue()` with updated `beads_status` and `beads_modified_at`
3. Call from `BidirectionalSyncWorkflow` in `temporal/workflows/bidirectional-sync.ts` after `syncBeadsToHuly()` succeeds
4. Also add writeback after `syncIssueToBeads()` in project-sync Phase 3

- `temporal/workflows/bidirectional-sync.ts` - call DB update after sync

**Problem**: 165 duplicate beads issues created for HVSYN project (368 total, 304 unique refs).

2. In `temporal/workflows/project-sync.ts` Phase 3 loop: Refresh `beadsIssues` list after each batch, or accumulate created IDs for dedup
3. Use `buildIssueLookups()` from `lib/BeadsDBReader.js` for O(1) dedup by title and Huly ID

description:
## Problem

In lib/beads/BeadsBatchSync.js line 72, the batch sync function calls db.getAllIssues() which loads ALL issues across ALL projects, even though only one project is being synced.

### Current code (line 72):
```js
const dbIssues = db.getAllIssues();  // SELECT * FROM issues ORDER BY identifier
const beadsIdToDbIssue = new Map(
  dbIssues.filter(i => i.beads_issue_id).map(i => [i.beads_issue_id, i])
);
```

Good: it caches in a Map (no N+1). Bad: fetches 1000+ rows when only 50 are relevant.

## Fix

Option A — Use project-scoped query (db.getProjectIssues already exists)
Option B — Add getIssuesWithBeadsId() to IssueRepository for SQL-level filtering

## Files to modify
- lib/beads/BeadsBatchSync.js (line 72)
- Optionally: lib/database/repositories/IssueRepository.js

## Verification
- npx vitest run tests/unit/BeadsService.test.js
- npx vitest run tests/unit/BeadsParentChildService.test.js

title: Cap WebhookDebouncer buffer to prevent memory leaks during high webhook traffic
description:
## Problem

WebhookDebouncer._pendingChanges (lib/webhook/WebhookDebouncer.js line 23) grows without limit. The buffer() method (line 37) pushes all incoming changes and only flushes after the debounce window (5000ms default).

During high webhook traffic (bulk imports, database migrations, webhook storms), hundreds of changes could accumulate before a single flush, consuming significant memory.

### Current code (lines 36-48):
```js
buffer(changes) {
  this._pendingChanges.push(...changes);  // No size limit
  if (this._debounceTimer) clearTimeout(this._debounceTimer);
  this._debounceTimer = setTimeout(() => this.flush(), this._debounceWindowMs);
}
```

Note: There IS a maxBatchSize (50) check at flush time (line 72), but it DISCARDS the oversized batch instead of splitting it — meaning large bursts lose data silently.

## Fix

1. Add emergency flush when buffer exceeds a hard cap (new _maxBufferSize param, default 500)
2. Change oversized batch behavior at flush to split into chunks instead of discarding
3. Add constructor param: maxBufferSize (default 500)

## Files to modify
- lib/webhook/WebhookDebouncer.js (buffer method + flush method + constructor)

## Verification
- npx vitest run tests/unit/HulyWebhookHandler.test.js

title: Batch issue processing (replace per-issue sequential awaits) with concurrency-limited pool

comments.author: vibesync
comments.text:
## Problem

Many sync operations are done per-issue sequentially (await per item), sometimes with added delays. This creates high wall time.

## Scope

- Identify per-issue loops in Phase 1/2 and Phase 3.
- Map API calls per issue (Huly update, Vibe update, Beads operations).

## Proposal

- Introduce a concurrency-limited worker pool (e.g., p-limit style) and process in chunks.
- Ensure ordering where required (status transitions, parent/child).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Large projects sync significantly faster.
- No increased mismatch/duplication.
- Rate limits respected.

## Risks

- API throttling / race conditions.

## Test Plan

- Implement pool with configurable concurrency.
- Run sync on known large dataset; compare timing.
- Validate counts match (issues created/updated) vs baseline.

description:
## Goal

- Scalar is a modern Swagger UI replacement with better mobile support and customization

title: Phase 1: OpenAPI schema extraction and spec generation
description:
## Goal
Generate OpenAPI 3.1 specification from Zod schemas in Vibesync's custom HTTP route registry.

## Why
- Current API documentation is manually maintained in docs/api/API.md
- Manual docs drift from implementation and require constant updates
- OpenAPI spec enables auto-generated UI (Scalar), client SDKs, and integration testing
- Zod v4.3.6 already in dependencies; zero framework migration needed

## Scope

### 1. Install zod-to-openapi
- Add @asteasolutions/zod-to-openapi@^8.5.0 to package.json
- Verify compatibility with Zod v4.3.6

### 2. Create src/openapi/ directory structure
- src/openapi/registry.ts — OpenAPI registry instance and route registration helpers
- src/openapi/schemas.ts — Centralized Zod schema definitions for request/response bodies
- src/openapi/routes.ts — Route metadata (path, method, tags, description) with schema bindings

### 3. Extract existing route schemas
- Audit src/api/routes/*.ts for existing Zod validators
- Consolidate validators into src/openapi/schemas.ts
- Register routes with OpenAPI metadata in src/openapi/routes.ts
- Start with high-value routes: projects.ts, issues.ts, health.ts

### 4. Generate OpenAPI spec
- Create scripts/generate-openapi.ts build script
- Script reads src/openapi/routes.ts and emits dist/openapi.json
- Add 'build:openapi' npm script
- Integrate into main 'build' script

### 5. Serve OpenAPI spec
- Update src/ApiServer.ts to serve /openapi.json endpoint
- Spec is served from dist/openapi.json (generated at build time)

## Tests
- Unit test: scripts/generate-openapi.ts produces valid OpenAPI 3.1 JSON
- Integration test: GET /openapi.json returns 200 with valid spec

## Done means
- @asteasolutions/zod-to-openapi installed and working
- src/openapi/ directory with registry, schemas, routes
- scripts/generate-openapi.ts generates valid OpenAPI spec
- GET /openapi.json endpoint returns spec
- High-value routes (projects, issues, health) documented in spec
- Tests passing

## Effort
2-3 days
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: OpenAPI 3.1 spec generation and /openapi.json endpoint implemented. All tests passing.

title: Auth on /formulas + /molecules routes
description:
Discovered while planning vibesync-0h2.

## Why

The MVP routes from vibesync-0h2.6 are unauthenticated — anyone with network reach to the ApiServer can spawn Letta agents on the configured backend, burning API credits and leaving teammate residue. Acceptable for the localhost dev loop; not OK for the docker-compose deployment topology referenced in README.md.

## Scope

- Decide on the auth model: bearer token in Authorization header (simplest), shared-secret env, or reuse whatever the existing project-registry routes use (audit src/api/routes/projects.ts first).
- Apply to POST /formulas/:name/run, GET /molecules/:id, GET /molecules/:id/events.
- 401 on missing/bad token; 403 on insufficient scope (if scopes are introduced).
- Read-only routes (GET /formulas, GET /molecules/:id) may have a separate, weaker auth; decide as part of this slice.

## Tests

routes.test.ts: missing header → 401; valid header → 202; bad header → 401.

## Done means

Routes ship with auth; .env.example documents the new var; deployment docs updated.

title: Real-server smoke test — drive code-review.toml end-to-end
description:
The real-server validator for the dispatcher journey, mirroring scripts/smoke/letta-teams-code-review.ts's role for vibesync-6wn.

## Why

Unit tests cover the dispatcher loop against FakeProvider; this slice proves the full plane against http://192.168.50.90:8289 (Letta 0.16.7), including: real teammate spawn for all three roles, real role-TOML memory-block seeding for each, real session events on the bus tagged by molecule_id + task_id, walker state mutations persisted to bd.

## Scope

- Split scripts/smoke/letta-teams-code-review.ts into two:
  - scripts/smoke/letta-teams-spawn.ts: regression-guard for the runtime path (current content of letta-teams-code-review.ts, single-prompt smoke).
  - scripts/smoke/formula-code-review.ts: NEW. Uses bootOrchestrationPlane (from .5) + dispatcher to drive packs/gastown/formulas/code-review.toml end-to-end against the gastown pack.
- The new script does not spawn agents directly — it constructs an OrchestrationHandle, calls dispatcher.run({ formula, pack, input: "review this change: <some-diff>" }) and watches the bus.
- Cleans up server-side agents on completion (mirrors the existing smoke's teardown step). Captures the run output in /tmp/formula-smoke.log for the closing commit message.

## Pass criteria

1. dispatcher/formula.started fires once; dispatcher/formula.completed fires once.
2. Three dispatcher/step.started → step.finished pairs (reviewer, coder, tester) in dep order.
3. Each step's spawned teammate has the role's [[memory_blocks]] from the pack TOML on its Letta agent (verify via client.agents.blocks.list).
4. walker.load(moleculeId) shows all three step beads in `closed` state with non-empty exec.output.
5. Drift auditor reports no findings for the molecule-namespaced teammates after teardown.

## Done means

bun scripts/smoke/formula-code-review.ts runs green; pass-criteria summary printed to stdout; results captured in the closing commit message for the epic.

Deps: blocked by .5 (needs bootOrchestrationPlane); benefits from .4 (memory blocks on coder/tester) — without .4, criterion 3 only validates the reviewer leg.
close_reason: Added formula-code-review smoke and letta-teams-spawn smoke entrypoint; fixed provider result mapping and dispatcher memfs defaults; bun scripts/smoke/formula-code-review.ts ran green with results in /tmp/formula-smoke.log.

title: API routes — POST /formulas/:name/run, GET /molecules/:id, SSE events
description:
Expose the dispatcher over HTTP so the CLI (and other clients) can use it.

## Scope

src/api/routes/formulas.ts (new):

  POST /formulas/:name/run
    body: { input: string, pack?: string, motivatingBeadId?: string }
    behavior: resolve pack (default "gastown") from packs/<pack>/, find formula by name across pack.formulas, kick off dispatcher.run() in the background (no await), return 202 with { moleculeId, formulaName } immediately.
    errors: 404 if pack/formula not found, 400 on missing input, 503 if orchestration plane not booted.

  GET /formulas
    response: { formulas: [{ name, pack, description, stepCount, roles[] }, ...] } across discoverable packs.

  GET /molecules/:id
    response: { moleculeId, formulaName, status, steps: [{ stepId, stepName, role, status, output? }, ...] }
    delegates to walker.load(rootId); 404 if not a molecule_root.

  GET /molecules/:id/events
    Server-Sent Events; subscribes to the orchestration bus, filters envelopes by molecule_id === :id, writes "event: <kind>\ndata: <json>\n\n" per match. Reuse src/api/SSEManager.ts.
    Auto-closes connection on dispatcher/formula.completed or dispatcher/formula.failed for that molecule.

Wiring:
- Register from src/ApiServer.ts only when app.orchestration is set (the OrchestrationHandle stash from .5).
- Route registration mirrors the existing registerXxxRoutes pattern in src/api/routes/.

## Tests (tests/unit/api/formulas.test.ts)

Use a stub dispatcher (the FakeProvider isn't enough here — we test the route layer, not the dispatcher).
- POST happy path returns moleculeId + 202.
- POST with missing input returns 400.
- POST with unknown formula returns 404.
- POST with no orchestration plane returns 503.
- GET /molecules/:id reflects walker state.
- SSE replays a recorded sequence of events, filters by molecule_id, closes on formula.completed.

## Done means

Type-check clean; formulas.test.ts green; ApiServer.ts gains a single new registerFormulaRoutes(...) call gated on app.orchestration.

Deps: blocked by .5.

title: Boot wiring — src/orchestration/boot.ts + ENABLE_ORCHESTRATION gate in src/index.ts
close_reason: Added bootOrchestrationPlane factory, ENABLE_ORCHESTRATION startup gate, orchestration handle plumbing into ApiServer deps, and boot tests; boot test and type-check pass.

title: Prompt template renderer (src/orchestration/dispatcher/render.ts)
description:
Pure-function template renderer. No dependencies; needed by .3.

## Why

Formula steps reference prompts via `prompt_template = "prompts/<name>.md"`. Role TOMLs do the same via `system_prompt_template = "prompts/<role>-system.md"`. Nothing in src/ currently loads and interpolates these files; the orchestration plane has no rendering layer at all.

## Scope

src/orchestration/dispatcher/render.ts:

  export interface RenderInput {
    readonly packRoot: string;
    readonly template: string;  // path relative to packRoot, e.g. "prompts/review.md"
    readonly context: Readonly<Record<string, string | number | boolean>>;
  }
  export function renderTemplate(input: RenderInput): string;

Conventions:
- Substitution syntax: `${name}`. No conditionals, loops, or filters — keep it loud and simple.
- Missing var throws. Silently leaving `${foo}` literal is a footgun; better to fail fast.
- Path-traversal protection: resolve(packRoot, template) must be under packRoot. Throws otherwise.
- Literal $ via `$$` → renders as `$`.

## Tests (tests/unit/orchestration/dispatcher/render.test.ts)

- Single + multiple substitutions.
- Missing var error includes the var name + template path.
- $$ escape.
- Path traversal rejected (template = "../../etc/passwd").
- Non-string ctx values coerce predictably (boolean → "true"/"false", number → toString).

## Done means

bunx vitest run tests/unit/orchestration/dispatcher/render passes; new dispatcher/ directory created with render.ts as its first file.
close_reason: Added src/orchestration/dispatcher/render.ts with traversal-safe  rendering plus tests; bunx vitest run tests/unit/orchestration/dispatcher/render.test.ts passes.
