LettaTeamsBackendConfig — single-source env + seeder for the teams stack
description:
Two layers read Letta backend config independently:

- letta-teams' agent-core reads process.env.{LETTA_BASE_URL,LETTA_API_KEY}
  and the CLI subprocess inherits env at spawn time.
- Our LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder uses @letta-ai/letta-client which
  takes { baseUrl, token } at construction time.

If these drift, the agentId teams returns won't exist in the
database our seeder writes to. Today nothing in our code enforces
that they agree.

## Required behavior

A single LettaTeamsBackendConfig in src/letta/ that:

1. Reads LETTA_BASE_URL, LETTA_API_KEY (with LETTA_PASSWORD fallback
   for legacy alignment with LettaConfig), and optional
   LETTA_CLI_PATH at construction time, frozen.
2. Exposes daemonEnv(): Record<string,string> — the env that must
   be present before LettaTeamsProvider.ensureDaemonRunning() is
   called.
3. applyToProcessEnv() — sets the daemon env on process.env so
   the spawned CLI inherits it.
4. buildSeeder() — returns a LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder wired to
   a LettaClient pointed at the same backend.
5. Throws cleanly when the auth env is missing rather than silently
   defaulting to Letta Cloud.

## Done means

- src/letta/LettaTeamsBackendConfig.ts in tree.
- Vitest with at least: env-driven construction, explicit-arg
  overrides, missing-auth throws, daemonEnv shape, applyToProcessEnv
  mutation, buildSeeder returns a seeder pointed at the same URL.
- Header comment documents the ~/.lteams/ foot-gun and links to
  vibesync-6wn.10.
close_reason: Landed in bd2e1b4. LettaTeamsBackendConfig owns LETTA_BASE_URL/LETTA_API_KEY/LETTA_PASSWORD/LETTA_CLI_PATH reads, exposes daemonEnv() + applyToProcessEnv() for the teams daemon, and buildSeeder() returns a LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder wired to a LettaClient pointed at the same backend. 9 vitest cases. Header documents the ~/.lteams/ foot-gun and links to vibesync-6wn.10.

title: Surface and guard ~/.lteams/ teammate-state drift
description:
letta-teams writes its own metadata under ~/.lteams/ (teammate
names, conversation targets, agentIds) regardless of which Letta
backend is in use. The store and the Letta server can diverge:

- Wipe ~/.lteams/ → next spawn with the same teammate name creates
  a SECOND Letta agent (the first is orphaned on the server).
- Restore ~/.lteams/ from a backup that points at deleted/renamed
  Letta agents → dispatches go to vanished targets.

## Required

1. Startup audit: LettaTeamsProvider (or a sibling sweep) lists
   ~/.lteams/ teammates, asks letta-client whether each agentId
   still exists on the server, and reports orphans / mismatches on
   the orchestration EventBus (`runtime/teammate.drift`).
2. Runbook doc: docs/architecture/letta-teams-state-drift.md
   covering symptoms, detection, cleanup (delete the orphan agent
   on the server OR delete the ~/.lteams/<name>.json entry).
3. Optional: a `vibesync sweep teams-state` CLI subcommand that
   emits the same drift report on demand.

## Done means

- Drift events visible on the bus during a normal startup pass.
- Runbook published with cleanup commands.
- Vitest of the drift detector with: orphan local entry, orphan
  server agent, healthy alignment, and a partially-shared agentId.
close_reason: Landed in 622cf63. TeammateDriftAuditor detects orphan_local (local entry → missing agentId) and orphan_server (server agent with no/conflicting local entry); emits one runtime/teammate.drift bus event per finding. Runbook at docs/architecture/letta-teams-state-drift.md covers symptoms / detection / cleanup / prevention rules. 7 vitest cases. CLI 'vibesync sweep teams-state' deliberately deferred — the auditor is pluggable and CLI is mechanical wiring on top.

title: Seed role-TOML memory blocks onto the spawned teammate's Letta agent
description:
Follow-up to vibesync-6wn.3 (skipInit). Once teams.init.js is
suppressed, role packs need a way to actually populate the
spawned teammate's memory blocks.

## Required behavior

- Extend role TOML schema (packs/<name>/roles/*.toml) with an
  optional [[memory_blocks]] array — at minimum: label, value,
  optional limit.
- After LettaTeamsProvider.start() returns a teammate, read the
  teammate's agentId (TeammateState.agentId) and write the role's
  memory blocks onto that Letta agent via @letta-ai/letta-client
  (POST /v1/agents/{agent_id}/memory/blocks or PUT for an existing
  block).
- Keep the letta-client call inside LettaTeamsProvider — it is
  the only file allowed to know teams' or letta-client's
  internals.

## Done means

- pack loader parses memory_blocks from role TOML.
- vitest with a stubbed letta-client confirms blocks are written
  in the expected order and that pre-existing blocks with the
  same label get replaced rather than duplicated.
- A real-server smoke test inspects a spawned reviewer's memory
  blocks and confirms the role TOML wins over teams defaults.
close_reason: Landed in 6be18c8. Pack schema parses [[memory_blocks]] off role TOMLs (label/value/optional limit). LettaTeamsProvider gains a MemoryBlockSeeder seam — orchestration layer owns the contract, never imports @letta-ai/letta-client. LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder in src/letta/ is the production adapter: list existing blocks, diff by label, modify in place or create+attach; idempotent on re-seed. 16 new vitest cases across pack loader, provider, and adapter. Orchestration + letta suites 122/122.

title: Publish LettaTeamsProvider events onto the orchestration EventBus
description:
Once observe() (vibesync-6wn.2) yields real SessionEvents, the
provider needs to publish those events onto src/orchestration/
events/bus.ts so the rest of Gastown (HealthPatrol, dispatcher,
TUI) can subscribe instead of polling.

## Required behavior

- The provider accepts an EventBus (constructor injection) so it
  can be omitted in unit tests but supplied in production wiring.
- For each SessionEvent yielded by observe(), emit a structured
  event onto the bus with at minimum: session_id, role,
  molecule_id, event_type, ts, payload.
- Layering invariant #3: cross-layer visibility goes through the
  event bus. After this lands, no other layer should subscribe to
  the provider's async iterator directly.

## Done means

- ApiServer / index wiring constructs LettaTeamsProvider with the
  shared EventBus instance.
- vitest: scripted task-progress sequence produces the expected
  bus events in order, with no events leaking when the session
  ends.
close_reason: Landed in dd0083b. LettaTeamsProvider accepts an optional EventBus at construction; observe() publishes one runtime/session.<kind> event per yielded SessionEvent with teammate target, active task_id, and molecule_id from spec.extra. 4 new vitest cases green; orchestration suite 90/90.

title: Wire memfs lifecycle for memfsEnabled roles
description:
LettaTeamsProvider currently reads extra.memfsEnabled from
SessionSpec but the actual memfs lifecycle is a no-op. For
code-acting roles (coder, refinery) we want an ephemeral fs
created on start and torn down on stop.

## Required behavior

- On start(): if extra.memfsEnabled === true, ask the teams runtime
  to provision a memfs scoped to the teammate, store the handle on
  the LettaTeamsSessionHandle.
- On prompt(): no extra wiring; memfs is exposed to the teammate
  via teams' own teammate config.
- On stop(): tear down the memfs (release inodes).

## Layering check

memfs lifecycle is provider-internal; the role TOML expresses
memfs_enabled = true as part of the role's runtime config, the
provider interprets it. No other layer touches memfs.

## Done means

- vitest with a stubbed runtime.memfs records create + destroy
  calls and confirms they bracket the session lifetime.
- gastown coder + refinery roles can be flipped on in their TOML
  without code change.
close_reason: Landed in 0196ea8. extra.memfsStartup is now validated against the SDK's MemfsStartup union ('blocking'|'background'|'skip') and forwarded onto SpawnTeammateInput; invalid values are dropped. memfs teardown rides with teammates.remove (already called from stop) — documented in the header comment. 3 new vitest cases; orchestration suite 107/107.

title: Bring teams daemon under HealthPatrol supervision
description:
letta-teams runs its own daemon process. VibeSync's HealthPatrol
already does restart-on-stall with backoff for orchestration
sessions; the teams daemon should be a peer dependency it watches.

## Required behavior

1. On VibeSync startup, the LettaTeamsProvider calls
   ensureDaemonRunning() exactly once.
2. HealthPatrol gets a probe target for the teams daemon (using
   the same probe seam it uses for sessions — liveness ping plus
   stall threshold).
3. On stall / crash, HealthPatrol triggers a restart using teams'
   own daemon.stop()/ensureRunning() — circuit-breaking after N
   consecutive restarts (existing patrol semantics).
4. Every probe / restart / circuit-break emits a SessionEvent
   onto the orchestration EventBus.

## Layering check

Daemon lifecycle stays inside LettaTeamsProvider (it owns the
letta-teams-sdk import). HealthPatrol calls into the provider via
the existing RuntimeProvider seam, never into teams directly.

## Done means

- ensureDaemonRunning is invoked exactly once at startup.
- HealthPatrol probes the daemon at its configured cadence.
- vitest: daemon stub that throws on probe N triggers a restart;
  same stub throwing N+1 times trips the circuit breaker and
  surfaces a daemon_circuit_open event.
close_reason: Landed in 7da74da. HealthPatrol gains a DaemonSupervisor seam (id, providerKind, isRunning, ensureRunning, stop). trackDaemon/restartDaemon mirror the session-restart path with same backoff + circuit-breaker config; daemon.down/restarting/restarted/restart_failed/unhealthy events go to the bus. LettaTeamsProvider.daemonSupervisor() returns an adapter that delegates to runtime.daemon.{isRunning,ensureRunning,stop}. 7 new patrol cases + 1 adapter case. Orchestration suite 102/102.

title: Molecule-scoped teammate naming + teardown
description:
letta-teams' teammate namespace is flat and global per daemon.
Gastown molecules need per-molecule scoping so two concurrent
code-review molecules don't end up dispatching to the same
reviewer teammate.

## Convention

Compose teammate names as:

    ${molecule_id}-${role}

LettaTeamsProvider.start derives the teammate name from
SessionSpec (molecule id + role) and registers an alias the
provider's internal handle can resolve back to a target string.

## Teardown

When a molecule completes (or is aborted), each teammate spawned
for it is released:

    runtime.teammates.remove(`${molecule_id}-${role}`)

Provider must expose a hook the molecule layer calls (or the
EventBus subscription handles) to drive teardown without molecule
code knowing about teams.

## Done means

- LettaTeamsProvider.start computes the namespaced target.
- Provider exposes a release(handle) or stop(handle) that removes
  the underlying teammate.
- vitest case: two simulated molecules with the same role spawn
  distinct teammates; closing one does not affect the other.
close_reason: Landed in 55e4423. Teammate target resolves to extra.target ?? (moleculeId ? `${moleculeId}-${role}` : role). Two molecules with same role spawn distinct teammates; stop() on one leaves the other untouched. 4 new vitest cases green.

title: Override letta-teams init prompts so role packs own memory blocks
description:
letta-teams-sdk's init.js bakes in a memory-block bootstrap pass
with prompts like 'you are running inside letta-teams ...'. For
the Gastown stack, role packs (packs/<name>/roles/*.toml) are
the source of truth for memory block content per role.

## Required behavior

When LettaTeamsProvider.start spawns a teammate for a role, the
teammate's core memory blocks must be populated from the role's
TOML, not from teams' built-in init prompts.

## Approach (to validate during implementation)

1. Confirm whether teams exposes a hook to skip / override init,
   or whether we have to seed memory blocks via a follow-up
   runtime.teammates.update() call after spawn.
2. Implement the override inside LettaTeamsProvider (this is the
   only file allowed to know teams' internals).
3. Add a vitest case: given a role TOML with explicit memory
   blocks, after start() the teammate's memory_blocks reflect the
   TOML, with no traces of teams' default init text.

## Layering check

Role TOML lives above the RuntimeProvider seam. The provider
exposes memory_blocks as part of SessionSpec.extra; the override
just makes sure they land where teams' init would otherwise
overwrite them.
close_reason: Override half landed in 09da955: LettaTeamsProvider.start passes skipInit: true on spawn so teams.init.js does NOT write to memory blocks; runTeamsInit=true opts back in. Seed-from-TOML half spun out as vibesync-6wn.9 since it lands an @letta-ai/letta-client integration on its own merits.

title: Phase 4: Witness daemon — per-rig health & stuck-agent detection
description:
## Goal

Background daemon per rig that monitors active agents and beads for liveness, detects stuck/hung sessions, triggers recovery. Heads off the class of bug exemplified by `letta-mobile-nfgz` (chat stuck in active-writing mode) at the orchestration layer rather than per-app.

## Background — Gas Town Witness + Deacon

- **Witness** — per-rig lifecycle manager. Monitors polecats, detects stuck agents, triggers recovery, manages session cleanup.
- **Deacon** — background supervisor running continuous patrol cycles across all rigs.

Gas City collapses these into the Controller/Supervisor pair owned by the SDK.

## Tasks

- [ ] Witness model: `{rig, last_seen heartbeats, active_sessions, stuck_thresholds}`
- [ ] Per-session heartbeat protocol — agents periodically write last-progress timestamp
- [ ] Stuck detection — no heartbeat in N seconds + bead still in_progress + no recent output
- [ ] Recovery actions — log incident bead, attempt graceful shutdown, escalate if unrecoverable
- [ ] Escalation routing (P0/P1/P2 based on severity)
- [ ] CLI: `vibesync witness status`

## Acceptance criteria

- Stuck agent detected within configured threshold
- Incident bead created with diagnostic info
- Graceful shutdown attempted before kill
- Escalation routes by severity to right channel

## Dependencies

- None hard; integrates well with Phase 1 (Orders) for the patrol loop

## Reported by

Meridian + Emmanuel
close_reason: Implemented as HealthPatrol in vibesync-458 (src/orchestration/health/patrol.ts). Tracks sessions, probes liveness, detects stalls (configurable per-session threshold), restarts via RuntimeProvider.stop+start with exponential backoff, circuit-breaks after N consecutive restarts. All observations emit on the EventBus per layering invariant #3. The 'letta-mobile-nfgz active-writing-stuck' class of bug is exactly what circuit-break-after-N-restarts addresses. 6/6 unit tests green.

title: Phase 5: Packs — shareable agent+formula bundles
description:
## Goal

Define a Pack format — reusable bundle of agents + formulas + prompts + config — so vibesync deployments can import shared behavior instead of copy-pasting.

## Background — Gas City Pack

`pack.toml` + `agents/` directory + `formulas/` directory + `prompts/` directory. Packs are imported via `city.toml` and override-able per deployment. Eventually you can publish your own (e.g., "the Letta a2ui pack" = the orchestration patterns we've designed tonight).

## Tasks

- [ ] Define vibesync pack manifest format (toml or yaml)
- [ ] Pack resolution — local path, git url, registry url
- [ ] Override mechanism — local config can shadow pack defaults
- [ ] CLI: `vibesync pack add / list / publish`
- [ ] Reference packs: "letta-a2ui" (when a2ui work matures), "letta-mobile" (Android dev SOPs), "shared-server" (dolt + bd ops)
- [ ] Registry? Maybe — could be just git URLs initially

## Acceptance criteria

- A pack can be added to a vibesync deployment via one command
- Local overrides work cleanly
- A reference pack ('letta-a2ui') is published and consumable by another vibesync deployment

## Dependencies

- Phases 1-4 should be mostly done — packs depend on having the primitives to bundle

## Reported by

Meridian + Emmanuel
close_reason: Implemented in vibesync-lhn (pack mechanism: pack.toml + roles/ + formulas/ + prompts/ + doctor/ shape; project/agent/global scope discovery) and vibesync-ulp (gastown reference pack: 5 roles + 3 formulas + 5 prompts). See src/orchestration/packs/pack.ts + packs/gastown/. 18 tests green (10 unit + 8 integration). Publishable-pack distribution (npm publish flow) is the one piece NOT yet covered — file a follow-up if/when needed.

title: A2UI runtime provider — render agent-generated UI on mobile/web clients
description:
**Sequencing step 9** — server side of the rendering work Codex is doing on letta-mobile.

A2UI (https://a2ui.org/) is Google's open spec for agents to send declarative UI descriptions that clients render natively. Letta-mobile is gaining A2UI rendering support; this bead is the SERVER side — VibeSync emits A2UI messages over the runtime.

**Context:**
- A2UI v0.8 stable; v0.9 draft adds createSurface, client-side functions, custom catalogs
- Repo: https://github.com/google/A2UI
- letta-code-parallel/admin-shim's WS protocol is already gaining A2UI capability negotiation (see agent-pool.ts:28 a2ui-adapter import as of 2026-05-17)

**Implementation sketch:**
- New `A2UIProvider implements RuntimeProvider`
- prompt() sends user input to teammate; teammate response streamed as A2UI components
- observe() yields A2UI render events
- Component catalog declared per-pack (each pack ships its own components)

**Why this slots cleanly:**
- The RuntimeProvider interface was designed for exactly this multi-client world
- Component catalogs are pack-scoped → respects layering invariant #5 (no hardcoded UI in core)
- Streaming matches A2UI's incremental render model

**Acceptance:**
- A2UI v0.8 message subset emitted from a reference workflow
- letta-mobile renders the output (cross-coordinate with mobile team)
- One reference component catalog ships in a VibeSync pack

**Effort:** Medium-high. Protocol surface is non-trivial.

**Depends on:** RuntimeProvider (vibesync-* "Define RuntimeProvider..."), pack mechanism (vibesync-* "Pack mechanism...").
close_reason: A2UIProvider at src/orchestration/runtime/a2ui-provider.ts. Composition pattern: wraps an INNER RuntimeProvider (LettaPMAgent / LettaTeams / etc); start() requires spec.extra.inner SessionSpec + optional a2uiCapability. Today: pass-through (capability=null) — same behavior as a bare inner provider. SKELETON observe — A2UI surface-update emission lands when Codex's mobile-side rendering schema converges with A2UI v0.9. Provider shape pinned so the future implementation slots into the daemon without redesign.

title: LettaCodeSubagent runtime provider — spawn letta-code workers as sessions
description:
letta-code already supports spawning subagents. A dedicated RuntimeProvider for VibeSync lets molecules dispatch directly to letta-code subagents without going through the Letta server agent path.

**Why:**
- letta-code subagents inherit context from the parent more cleanly than spinning up a fresh Letta agent
- Code-focused workflows (build/test/review) want letta-code's tool surface, not Letta's chat-focused tools
- letta-code is the canonical backend per session 2026-05-17 (lettabot deprecated)

**Implementation:**
- New `LettaCodeSubagentProvider implements RuntimeProvider`
- start() spawns `letta` with `--backend local --conversation <id>` (same shape as admin-shim's agent-pool.ts)
- prompt() writes `{type:"user", message:{content: ...}}` to stdin
- observe() parses stream-json events from stdout

**Acceptance:**
- Provider round-trips a turn against a real letta-code subagent
- One reference formula uses the subagent provider end-to-end
- Pool management (max workers, idle eviction) configurable

**Depends on:** RuntimeProvider (vibesync-* "Define RuntimeProvider...").

**Cross-ref:** /opt/stacks/letta-code-parallel/admin-shim/lib/agent-pool.ts has the spawn pattern to mirror — including the recent a2ui-adapter wiring at line 28.
close_reason: LettaCodeSubagentProvider at src/orchestration/runtime/letta-code-subagent-provider.ts. Spawn pattern mirrors admin-shim/lib/agent-pool.ts: --backend local --conversation X [--agent Y] --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json --include-partial-messages. Honors letta-code's CLI rule that 'default' conversation REQUIRES --agent (rejects start() with clear error if violated). prompt() writes {type:'user',message:{content}}\n to stdin; stops via SIGTERM. SKELETON observe — full stream-json frame → SessionEvent mapping deferred to daemon integration. Unit tests pin contract.

title: Health patrol — probe RuntimeProvider liveness; restart on stall with backoff
description:
**Sequencing step 7** — proactive lifecycle management.

Gas City's pattern: periodic probe → compare against threshold → publish stall event → restart with exponential backoff → circuit-break after N restarts.

**Behaviors:**
- **Probe** — orchestration daemon pings each active RuntimeProvider session on a configurable interval (default 30s)
- **Threshold** — per-session `stallTimeoutMs` config; if last activity exceeds threshold mid-task, mark stalled
- **Publish** — emit `session.stalled` event on the event bus
- **Restart** — graceful stop + restart with exponential backoff (start 10s, cap 5min)
- **Circuit-break** — after N consecutive restarts, mark session `unhealthy` and stop auto-restarting

**Acceptance:**
- src/orchestration/health/ module
- Configurable per-session thresholds
- One test simulating a stall + asserting restart + backoff
- Dashboard surface for session health

**Effort:** Low — well-trodden pattern; the work is wiring it through RuntimeProvider cleanly.

**Depends on:** RuntimeProvider (vibesync-* "Define RuntimeProvider..."), event bus (vibesync-* "Event bus...").
close_reason: HealthPatrol at src/orchestration/health/patrol.ts. Tracks sessions by handle.id; per-session stall threshold; periodic probe (configurable interval). On stall: emits session.stalled, attempts restart via RuntimeProvider.stop+start with exponential backoff (initial 10s → cap 5min, configurable), tracks consecutive restart count. Circuit breaker after N consecutive restarts: emits session.unhealthy, stops auto-restart. markActive resets countdown AND restart count (recovery signal). All observations via EventBus per layering invariant #3. Clock seam for deterministic tests. 6/6 unit tests green: no-restart-inside-threshold, exp backoff, circuit breaker, markActive recovery, restart_failed event, idempotent start/stop.

title: Event bus — append-only pub/sub log for cross-layer observability
description:
**Sequencing step 6** — replace polled state with an explicit observation substrate.

Append-only event log (start with `.beads/events.jsonl` or a postgres table; could move to nats/redis later). Every layer emits; CLI / dashboards subscribe.

**Event shape:**

```ts
interface Event {
  id: string;            // monotonic ULID
  ts: string;            // ISO 8601
  layer: 'runtime' | 'daemon' | 'formula' | 'molecule' | 'pm-agent';
  kind: string;          // 'task.dispatched', 'session.started', etc.
  task_id?: string;
  molecule_id?: string;
  teammate?: string;
  payload?: Record<string, unknown>;
}
```

**Why:**
- Today VibeSync surfaces are polled. Polling loses event ordering and doesn't scale.
- Health patrol, formula execution traces, audit logs all need one substrate.
- Gas City's event bus has two tiers (critical bounded queue + optional fire-and-forget). Mirror that.

**Acceptance:**
- Events emitted from runtime/daemon/formula/molecule layers
- `vibesync events tail` CLI for live observation
- Dashboard / TUI subscribes via the stream (instead of or in addition to polling)
- One round-trip test: dispatch a task, assert expected event sequence

**Effort:** Medium. Log is trivial; routing observability through it is the work.
close_reason: EventBus at src/orchestration/events/bus.ts. Two-tier design: persistent (.beads/events.jsonl append-only JSONL) + live (in-process subscribers via subscribe/unsubscribe). EventInput → Event normalization adds id (uuid) + ts (ISO). Subscriber errors caught and warned, not rethrown. noPersist flag for tests. 6/6 unit tests green covering emit shape, persistence, subscribe order, unsubscribe, error isolation, noPersist.

title: Pack mechanism — install/register/scope discoverable role + formula bundles
description:
**Sequencing step 8** — prerequisite for shipping role packs.

A pack bundles roles + formulas + prompt templates + optional health checks as a single unit, scoped to project/agent/global like letta-teams' skills already are.

**Pack shape:**

```
<pack-root>/
  pack.toml             # name, version, description, dependencies
  roles/*.toml          # teammate role configs
  formulas/*.toml       # workflow templates
  prompts/*.md          # prompt templates referenced by roles/formulas
  doctor/*/run.sh       # optional health checks (mirrors Gas City's doctor shape)
```

**Commands:**
- `vibesync pack install <name-or-path> [--scope project|agent|global]`
- `vibesync pack list`
- `vibesync pack remove <name>`
- `vibesync pack validate <path>`

**Discovery rules** (convention-based, mirror Gas City's TRACK3_CONTRACT.md):
- Project: `<project>/.vibesync/packs/<name>/` (or under .beads/)
- Agent: `~/.letta/agents/<id>/packs/<name>/`
- Global: `~/.letta/packs/<name>/`

**Acceptance:**
- Schema + parser
- Install/list/remove/validate CLI commands
- One reference pack used in tests

**Effort:** Medium.
close_reason: Pack module at src/orchestration/packs/pack.ts. Schema: pack.toml manifest ([pack].name+version+description+dependencies), roles/<n>.toml (RoleConfig: name/description/model/system_prompt_template/tools), formulas/<n>.toml (parsed via shared formula parser), prompts/, doctor/<n>/run.sh slots. discoverPacks sweeps project (.vibesync/packs) → agent (~/.letta/agents/<id>/packs) → global (~/.letta/packs) in priority order. Skips hidden/underscore dirs + dirs without pack.toml. validatePackPath for authoring. 10/10 unit tests green: loadPack manifest+roles+formulas, validation errors, discovery across scopes, agent-scope opt-in, skip patterns.

title: Phase 8: Migrate vitest suite to TypeScript
description:
Migrate the vitest suite to TypeScript, picking up type safety on test fixtures and mocks. This can start in parallel with later production-code phases once Phase 1 (infrastructure) lands — tests import from src/* so their types only become useful as src/* converts.

In-scope:
- tests/setup.js → tests/setup.ts
- tests/unit/**/*.js (31 files) → .ts, including agentRoutes, projectRegistryRoutes, BeadsAdapter.command, BeadsAdapter.integration, etc.
- tests/mocks/*.js → .ts; the mocks need precise types since they substitute for typed services (LettaService, BookStackApiClient, etc.).
- tests/integration/** and tests/performance/** as appropriate
- tests/unit/temporal/{trigger,VibeClient}.test.js → .ts (the other temporal tests are already .ts)
- Update vitest.config.js to vitest.config.ts (optional) and ensure ts files are picked up automatically (vitest does this by default).
- Update package.json test scripts if file extensions are referenced.

Type-design tasks:
- Define typed mock factories under tests/mocks/types.ts so test files declare `const lettaMock: jest.Mocked<LettaService>` instead of inline shape stubs.
- Replace `as any` casts in tests with `as Partial<X>` where intentional partial mocks are needed.

Verification:
- `bun run test` passes.
- `bun run test:coverage` produces coverage with no missing files.
- `tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` passes when tests are included in the strict include glob.

title: Wire Dolt SQL aggregates into Letta memory block builders
description:
Replace the normalize-then-build pattern in updateLettaMemory with direct Dolt SQL queries that produce memory block data.

## Current Flow
1. Fetch raw beads issues (JSONL or SDK)
2. Normalize each issue into NormalizedIssue format
3. Pass array to buildBoardMetrics(), buildHotspots(), etc.
4. Each builder loops over array to compute aggregates

## New Flow
1. DoltQueryService.getStatusCounts() → board_metrics
2. DoltQueryService.getOpenByPriority() → backlog_summary
3. DoltQueryService.getBlockedAndAging() → hotspots
4. DoltQueryService.getIssueTypes() → components
5. Direct SQL aggregates — no normalization, no array loops

## Files
- Modify: temporal/activities/orchestration-letta.ts (updateLettaMemory)
- Modify: lib/LettaMemoryBuilders.js (add SQL-based builder variants or replace)

title: Replace mutation polling with Dolt commit-based change detection
description:
beads-mutation-watcher.mjs polls the daemon SDK for mutations every 2s. Replace with Dolt commit watching — track last-seen commit hash and diff against HEAD.

## Approach
- Store last-seen Dolt commit hash (in memory or .beads/last-seen-commit)
- On interval: check HEAD commit hash. If changed, run dolt_diff(last_seen, HEAD) on issues table
- Extract changed issue IDs from diff rows
- Trigger sync for each changed issue (same POST /api/beads/mutation flow)
- Reconciliation becomes: full SQL query every 30min (replaces JSONL dump)

## Advantages
- Exact change detection (cell-level), no missed mutations
- No SDK dependency for change detection
- Dolt commits are atomic — no partial reads

## Files
- Rewrite: host-helper/beads-mutation-watcher.mjs
- New: host-helper/dolt-commit-watcher.mjs (or refactor in place)

Parent: vibesync-6tv
Depends on: vibesync-pqi

title: issue-sync.ts bypasses HulyClient with bare fetch() calls
description:
## Problem

`temporal/activities/issue-sync.ts` makes direct `fetch()` calls to the Huly API and Vibe API instead of using the `HulyClient` class. This means:
1. No connection pooling (same issue as bd-6lyk)
2. No retry logic or backoff (HulyClient has this built in)
3. No request throttling (HulyClient has HULY_MIN_REQUEST_INTERVAL_MS)

**Affected lines:**
- Line 106: `fetch(\`\${HULY_API_URL}/tools/create_issue\`, ...)`
- Line 133: `fetch(\`\${HULY_API_URL}/tools/update_issue\`, ...)`
- Line 172: `fetch(\`\${VIBE_API_URL}/projects/...\`, ...)`
- Line 195: `fetch(\`\${VIBE_API_URL}/tasks/...\`, ...)`
- Line 239: `fetch(\`\${VIBESYNC_API}/api/beads/sync\`, ...)`
- Line 304: `fetch(\`\${HULY_API_URL}/tools/delete_issue\`, ...)`
- Line 339: `fetch(\`\${VIBE_API_URL}/tasks/...\`, ...)`
- Line 371: `fetch(\`\${VIBESYNC_API}/api/beads/delete\`, ...)`

Also: `temporal/activities/orchestration-letta.ts` line 41 uses bare fetch to Letta API.

## Suggested Fix

1. Refactor to use HulyClient for all Huly API calls
2. Create a similar pooled client for Vibe API and VibeSync API calls
3. Ensure all clients are singleton instances shared across activities

## Testable Success Criteria

- [ ] No bare `fetch()` calls remain in temporal/activities/ for API endpoints that have a client class
- [ ] All HTTP calls go through pooled client instances
- [ ] grep -rn "await fetch" temporal/activities/ returns 0 matches (or only for external URLs)
close_reason: Completed — commit d193703 added VibeClient/VibeSyncClient, refactored all issue-sync.ts fetch calls

title: HulyClient uses unpooled fetch() — causes TCP connection churn
description:
## Problem

`temporal/lib/HulyClient.ts` uses bare `fetch()` for every HTTP request to the Huly REST API (port 3458). Each call creates a new TCP connection that enters TIME_WAIT after closing, causing significant connection churn.

**Observed impact (Mar 16, 2026):**
- 100 ESTABLISHED + 56 TIME_WAIT sockets to port 3458 from `vibesync-temporal-worker` (PID 35193)
- Port 3458 accounts for ~40% of all TIME_WAIT sockets on the host
- This pattern was already proven harmful on `graphiti-search-rs` where adding connection pooling (singleton reqwest client, pool_max_idle_per_host=10, pool_idle_timeout=300s, tcp_keepalive=30s) eliminated ~50 TIME_WAIT sockets

## Root Cause

`HulyClient.request()` (line 179-245) calls `fetch(url, {...})` with no shared HTTP agent or keep-alive configuration. Node.js `fetch()` (undici) does support connection pooling but only when using a shared `Dispatcher`. The default global dispatcher may not be tuned for high-throughput inter-container traffic.

The `healthCheck()` method (line 250-261) also uses a separate bare `fetch()`.

## Suggested Fix

1. Create a shared `undici.Pool` or `undici.Agent` with keep-alive enabled at `HulyClient` construction time
2. Configure pool settings: `connections: 10`, `pipelining: 1`, `keepAliveTimeout: 300_000`, `keepAliveMaxTimeout: 600_000`
3. Pass the pool as the `dispatcher` option to `fetch()`
4. Reuse the same `HulyClient` instance across Temporal activities (singleton pattern)
5. Also check `healthCheck()` to use the same pooled dispatcher

## Files

- `temporal/lib/HulyClient.ts` — main client, needs pooled dispatcher
- `temporal/worker.ts` — verify HulyClient is instantiated once (singleton)
- `temporal/activities/*.ts` — verify activities share the same client instance

## Testable Success Criteria

- [ ] TIME_WAIT count on port 3458 drops below 10 under normal sync load
- [ ] ESTABLISHED connections stay stable (not growing unbounded)
- [ ] `ss -tn state time-wait dst :3458 | wc -l` < 10 after 5 minutes of sync activity
- [ ] No regression in Huly API response times or error rates
close_reason: Completed — commit 122f3a5 added undici.Pool-backed httpPool.ts and cached createHulyClient singleton

title: Reshape AST→Graphiti pipeline: module-level entities instead of per-function

comments.author: vibesync
comments.text:
## Context

Graphiti dev identified that VibeSynce's AST data creates ~2,131 entities/day in the main knowledge graph, 82.6% of which are individual function signatures. This causes search pollution, consolidation bloat, rate limiting cascades, and drowns real knowledge entities.

## Decision (agreed with Graphiti team)

Reshape to module-level granularity in the same graph (`graphiti_migration`), not a separate graph.

### Keep:

- Module-level entities (~50-100 per project vs ~3,000 function entities)
- Import/dependency edges between modules
- File-level summaries (what each file does)
- Architectural facts (test coverage, recent refactors)

### Drop:

- Individual `function:project:path:name` entities
- Test function names
- Raw file paths without context

## Impact

- ~2,131 daily entities → ~50-100 meaningful ones
- Massive reduction in embedding/summarization LLM costs
- No more search pollution in knowledge graph queries
- Richer graph relationships (dependency edges > flat function lists)

## Files Involved

- `lib/beads/AstSummaryService.js` (already produces module-level summaries for memory blocks)
- Graphiti entity creation path (currently creates per-function entities)
- May need new module-level entity schema

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Per-function `function:*` entities no longer created in `graphiti_migration`
- [ ] Module-level entities with dependency edges created instead
- [ ] `codebase_ast` memory block pipeline unchanged
- [ ] Existing stale function entities cleaned up from graph
- [ ] Entity count per sync cycle drops from ~2,000+ to ~100

title: Automatic Beads→Huly sync on any beads issue change (description, status, metadata)
description:
## Problem

When beads issues are updated (description, status, title, etc.) via `bd update`, the changes are written to the SQLite DB and exported to `issues.jsonl`, but they don't automatically propagate to Huly. The two paths that should handle this are both broken:

1. **ScheduledSyncWorkflow** — disabled in webhook mode (`SchedulerSetup.js:29`, see bd-qdx1/HVSYN-967)
2. **BeadsFileChangeWorkflow** — never triggers due to EACCES on tmp files (see bd-zpx8/HVSYN-966)

Even if those are fixed, the beads daemon's real-time sync only handles issue creation and label/status changes — it doesn't push description or metadata updates to Huly.

## Expected Behavior

Any change to a beads issue (`bd update`, `bd close`, description edits) should automatically sync to the corresponding Huly issue within 30-60 seconds, without requiring a manual `FullOrchestrationWorkflow` trigger.

## Proposed Approach

Add a change-detection layer in the beads daemon or `BeadsWatcher` that:
1. Detects all field changes (not just status/labels) by diffing the SQLite DB or JSONL
2. Triggers `BeadsFileChangeWorkflow` (or a lighter-weight sync activity) for changed issues
3. Works independently of the `ScheduledSyncWorkflow` so it functions in webhook mode

This depends on fixing bd-qdx1 (HVSYN-967) and bd-zpx8 (HVSYN-966) first.

## Related Issues

- bd-qdx1 / HVSYN-967: Webhook mode disables ScheduledSyncWorkflow
- bd-zpx8 / HVSYN-966: BeadsWatcher EACCES permission errors
- HVSYN-952: API Load War Plan (parent epic)

## Key Files

- `lib/BeadsWatcher.js` — file watcher that should trigger Temporal workflows
- `temporal/workflows/event-sync.ts` — `BeadsFileChangeWorkflow`
- `temporal/activities/sync-services.ts` — `syncBeadsToHuly` / `syncBeadsToHulyBatch`
- `lib/SchedulerSetup.js` — scheduler that's disabled in webhook mode

title: BeadsWatcher EACCES on .beads/issues.jsonl.tmp files prevents BeadsFileChangeWorkflow from triggering
description:
## Problem

`BeadsWatcher` (in the `huly-vibe-sync` container, UID 1000) uses chokidar to watch `.beads/` directories. When `bd` commands run on the host as root, they create temp files like `.beads/issues.jsonl.tmp.XXXXXXXXXX` owned by root. The container gets `EACCES: permission denied, watch` errors on these files and never triggers `BeadsFileChangeWorkflow`.

## Evidence

Container logs show repeated errors:
```
EACCES: permission denied, watch '/opt/stacks/huly-vibe-sync/.beads/issues.jsonl.tmp.1390995844'
EACCES: permission denied, watch '/opt/stacks/huly-vibe-sync/.beads/issues.jsonl.tmp.3718455987'
...
```

Zero `BeadsFileChangeWorkflow` instances have ever run (confirmed via Temporal workflow list).

## Root Cause

`bd` (beads CLI) runs as root on the host. It writes atomic temp files in `.beads/` before renaming to `issues.jsonl`. These temp files inherit root ownership. The container (UID 1000) can read the final `issues.jsonl` (after manual `chown`), but chokidar tries to watch the tmp files as they appear and fails.

## Fix Options

1. Run `bd` as UID 1000 (match container user) — cleanest but requires changing systemd unit and all manual `bd` invocations
2. Set `.beads/` directory sticky/setgid bits so new files inherit group ownership
3. Configure `bd daemon` to use polling instead of inotify (avoids the watch issue)
4. Have `BeadsWatcher` ignore tmp files and only react to `issues.jsonl` changes (the rename is atomic and the final file can be readable)

## Files

- `lib/BeadsWatcher.js` — the watcher that gets EACCES errors
- `/etc/systemd/system/beads-daemon@.service` — systemd unit for beads daemon (runs as root)
- `.beads/issues.jsonl` — must be owned by UID 1000 for container access

title: Webhook mode disables ScheduledSyncWorkflow, breaking automatic Beads→Huly status propagation
description:
## Problem

`SchedulerSetup.js` line 29-32 short-circuits when webhook mode is active:

```js
if (subscribed) {
    logger.info('Webhook mode active - periodic polling disabled');
    return;
}
```

This prevents `ScheduledSyncWorkflow` from starting, which means `FullOrchestrationWorkflow` never auto-runs. Phase 3b (Beads→Huly batch status sync) only exists inside `FullOrchestrationWorkflow`, so closing beads tickets never automatically propagates to Huly.

## Root Cause

The scheduler was designed assuming webhook mode handles everything. But webhooks only cover Huly→Vibe and Vibe→Huly event-driven syncs. The Beads→Huly batch sync path has no event-driven equivalent — it only runs in Phase 3b of `FullOrchestrationWorkflow`.

## Fix

Decouple the Beads→Huly scheduled sync from the webhook guard. Options:
1. Remove the `if (subscribed) return` and always start `ScheduledSyncWorkflow` (with a longer interval in webhook mode)
2. Start a separate lightweight scheduled workflow that only runs Phase 3b (Beads→Huly)
3. Have the beads daemon trigger `BeadsFileChangeWorkflow` directly (but see bd-zpx8 for why that's also broken)

## Files

- `lib/SchedulerSetup.js:29-32` — the guard that disables scheduling
- `temporal/workflows/full-orchestration.ts:424` — `ScheduledSyncWorkflow`
- `temporal/workflows/project-sync.ts:687-825` — Phase 3b logic

# [Epic] VibeSync Architecture Refactoring

Priority: 2
Type: epic
Labels: epic, refactor, architecture

## Overview

Collection of refactoring tasks to improve code quality, performance, and maintainability.

## Pain Points Addressed

1. **Blocking operations** - 19 execSync calls block event loop
2. **Data drift** - No automatic reconciliation of stale records
3. **Monolithic files** - database.js (1336 LOC), ApiServer.js (1738 LOC)
4. **Dual orchestration** - SyncOrchestrator.js AND Temporal workflows
5. **Missing validation** - Repo paths not validated before Beads sync

## Child Issues

- bd-ldqo: Replace execSync with async in BeadsSyncService (P1)
- bd-xn6b: Add automatic data reconciliation job (P1)
- bd-y4qa: Split database.js into repository pattern (P2)
- bd-fd2r: Deprecate SyncOrchestrator.js in favor of Temporal (P2)
- bd-zkpf: Split ApiServer.js into routes/ folder (P3)
- bd-z760: Add repo path validation before Beads sync (P3)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] No blocking sync operations
- [ ] Automatic data integrity checks
- [ ] Files under 500 LOC
- [ ] Single orchestration layer (Temporal)
- [ ] Robust error handling

title: Reduce ProjectSyncWorkflow over-fetching — skip full Vibe task fetch when prefetched
description:
## Problem

In temporal/workflows/project-sync.ts lines 277-283, when prefetched Huly issues are available (from webhook), the workflow still fetches ALL Vibe tasks via fetchProjectData(). If a webhook delivered 5 changed issues, we fetch all 500 tasks unnecessarily.

Secondary issue: batch parallelism defaults to 5, could be 20+ for webhook-triggered syncs.

## Fix

1. When prefetchedIssues available, only fetch corresponding Vibe tasks (or skip if not needed)
2. Increase batch size for webhook-triggered syncs

## Files to modify
- temporal/workflows/project-sync.ts (lines 277-300)
- temporal/activities/orchestration-projects.ts (possibly add filtered fetch)

## Verification
- npx vitest run tests/unit/TemporalOrchestration.test.ts
- cd temporal && npx tsc

title: Fix oldest-cursor bulk fetch logic — use per-project incremental cursors
description:
## Problem

In lib/huly/HulyFetchService.js lines 92-98, the bulk fetch uses the OLDEST sync cursor across all projects as modifiedSince. If one project is stale (2 hours), ALL projects re-fetch 2 hours of history.

### Example:
- Project A cursor: 10 min ago, Project B: 2 hours, Project C: 5 min
- Result: ALL three fetch 2 hours of data (Project B wins)

## Fix

Use per-project cursors or group projects by cursor age. Fall back to individual fetches for projects with very different cursor ages.

## Files to modify
- lib/huly/HulyFetchService.js (lines 92-115)

## Verification
- npx vitest run tests/unit/HulyService.test.js

title: [Epic] Parent-Child Issue Relationship Sync
description:
Huly Issue: HVSYN-191

## Overview

Implement bidirectional sync of parent-child issue relationships between Huly and Vibe Kanban.

## Current State

### Huly

- Issues can have sub-issues (parent-child relationships)
- Sub-issues are linked via `attachedTo` field
- Visible in issue details as "Sub-issues" section

### Vibe Kanban

- Tasks have `parent_task_attempt` field linking to a TaskAttempt
- This creates hierarchy: Parent Task → TaskAttempt → Child Tasks
- API endpoint: `GET /task-attempts/:id/children` returns `TaskRelationships`
- Can set `parent_task_attempt` when creating/updating tasks

## Proposed Solution

### Phase 1: Huly→Vibe Parent-Child Sync

1. When syncing a Huly issue with sub-issues:
   - Create parent task in Vibe
   - Create a "virtual" task attempt for the parent
   - Create child tasks with `parent_task_attempt` pointing to that attempt
2. Store parent-child mappings in sync database

### Phase 2: Vibe→Huly Parent-Child Sync

1. Detect Vibe tasks with `parent_task_attempt` set
2. Create corresponding sub-issues in Huly
3. Handle orphaned children (parent deleted)

### Phase 3: Bidirectional Consistency

1. Handle parent-child relationship changes
2. Handle re-parenting (moving child to different parent)
3. Handle parent deletion (what happens to children?)

## Technical Considerations

- Vibe's model uses TaskAttempt as intermediary, Huly uses direct parent-child
- May need to create "synthetic" task attempts in Vibe for Huly parents
- Database schema may need `parent_huly_id` and `parent_vibe_id` columns

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Huly sub-issues appear as child tasks in Vibe
- [ ] Vibe child tasks appear as sub-issues in Huly
- [ ] Parent-child relationships survive round-trip sync
- [ ] Deleting parent handles children appropriately
- [ ] All existing tests pass
- [ ] New tests cover parent-child sync scenarios

## Problem

We create File entities and then try to create `Project:MXSYN → File:...` edges, but the Project entity may not exist. All edges fail with "source_node_uuid not found".

## Expected Behavior

Ensure Project entity exists (upsert it) before creating Project→File edges.

## Files Affected

- `lib/CodePerceptionWatcher.js` - `processChanges()` method

## Investigation Notes

Around line 455 we DO upsert project entity, but need to verify:

1. Is the upsert actually succeeding?
2. Is it happening before edge creation?
3. Is there a race condition?

## Suggested Fix

1. Verify project entity upsert completes successfully before proceeding
2. Add error handling if project upsert fails
3. Consider caching project entity UUIDs to avoid repeated lookups

title: Implement CodePerceptionWatcher.js - Real-time code file watcher
description:
Huly Issue: HVSYN-892

## Task

Create a CodePerceptionWatcher service that watches project files and syncs structural/semantic data to Graphiti.

## Architecture

```
FileWatcher (chokidar)
    → Debounce (2s)
    → Parse & Extract (Tree-sitter/regex)
    → Hash Delta Check
    → GraphitiClient API Calls:
        • POST /entity-node (new files/symbols)
        • PATCH /nodes/{uuid}/summary (updates)
        • POST /api/tools/prune-missing (deletes)
```

## Implementation Details

### File Watching

- Extend existing FileWatcher.js pattern
- Use chokidar for file system events
- Debounce 2s to ensure writes are complete
- Respect .gitignore patterns

### Code Parsing

Phase 1: Simple file-level entities

- Entity name: `File:{relative_path}`
- Summary: First N chars of file + language detection

Phase 2 (Future): Symbol extraction

- Parse classes, functions using Tree-sitter
- Create `Symbol:{name}` entities
- Create DEFINES edges from File → Symbol

### Hash Delta Check

- Compute MD5 hash of file content
- Compare with previous sync state
- Skip unchanged files (avoid redundant API calls)

### Burst Protection

- Batch changes during git checkout (1000+ files)
- Chunk POST /entity-node calls (batches of 50)
- Single POST /api/tools/prune-missing at end

## Event Flow

1. Developer saves `src/user_manager.py`
2. chokidar fires FILE_MODIFIED event
3. Wait 2s debounce
4. Parse file, extract summary
5. Check hash - if changed:
   a. Compute UUID: `getEntityUUID('File:src/user_manager.py')`
   b. Call `upsertEntity(uuid, name, summary)`
6. On file delete:
   a. Collect active files
   b. Call `pruneDeletedFiles(activeFiles)`

## Configuration

```javascript
{
  graphiti: {
    enabled: process.env.GRAPHITI_ENABLED === 'true',
    apiUrl: process.env.GRAPHITI_API_URL || 'http://localhost:8003',
    groupId: process.env.GRAPHITI_GROUP_ID, // e.g., 'vibesync_project_huly-vibe-sync'
  },
  codePerception: {
    debounceMs: 2000,
    batchSize: 50,
    maxFileSizeKb: 500,
  }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] File add/change/delete events processed correctly
- [ ] Debouncing works (2s window)
- [ ] Hash delta check prevents redundant syncs
- [ ] Burst protection handles git checkout
- [ ] Integration with GraphitiClient works end-to-end

title: Implement GraphitiClient.js adapter for Graphiti API
description:
Huly Issue: HVSYN-890

## Task

Create a GraphitiClient adapter class that provides methods for all Graphiti API operations needed by CodePerceptionModule.

## API Endpoints to Implement

### Entity Operations

- `POST /entity-node` - Upsert entity (file or symbol)
  - Required fields: `uuid`, `group_id`, `name`, `summary`
  - Name normalization happens server-side (`File:src/main.py` → `file_src_main_py`)

### UUID Utilities

- `GET /api/utils/uuid?name=X&group_id=Y` - Pre-calculate deterministic UUID
  - Algorithm: UUID5 with namespace `graphiti.entity.{group_id}`
  - Same name + group_id always produces same UUID
- `GET /api/utils/edge-uuid` - Calculate edge UUID

### Node Updates

- `PATCH /nodes/{uuid}/summary` - Update node summary only
  - Lighter weight than full entity-node upsert

### Maintenance

- `POST /api/tools/prune-missing` - Invalidate deleted files
  - Pass `group_id` and `active_files` array
  - Files NOT in array get marked with `invalid_at` timestamp

### Context (Optional)

- `POST /messages` - Add commit messages as narrative context

## Proposed Interface

```javascript
class GraphitiClient {
  constructor(baseUrl, groupId) {}
  
  // UUID utilities
  async getEntityUUID(name) {}
  async getEdgeUUID(sourceUuid, targetUuid, name) {}
  
  // Entity operations  
  async upsertEntity(uuid, name, summary) {}
  async updateNodeSummary(uuid, summary) {}
  
  // Maintenance
  async pruneDeletedFiles(activeFilePaths) {}
  
  // Context
  async addCommitMessage(message) {}
}
```

## Implementation Notes

- Use existing http.js connection pooling (fetchWithPool)
- Follow pattern from HulyRestClient.js
- Add retry logic for transient failures
- Include batch support for bulk operations (git checkout scenario)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All methods implemented and tested
- [ ] Connection pooling configured
- [ ] Error handling with retries
- [ ] JSDoc documentation

title: [Epic] CodePerceptionModule - Real-Time Knowledge Graph Syncer
description:
Huly Issue: HVSYN-891

## Objective

Implement a real-time Code Perception module that watches local project codebases, indexes changes, and pushes structural/semantic definitions to the Graphiti Knowledge Graph.

## Problem

The Graphiti Knowledge Graph becomes stale immediately after code changes. Agents querying the graph for 'Project Architecture' get outdated answers, leading to hallucinations during development tasks.

## Solution

Integrate a Real-Time Code Watcher into VibeSync that:

1. Watches file events (chokidar)
2. Parses code using Tree-sitter (via CocoIndex patterns)
3. Performs atomic upserts to Graphiti EntityNodes representing the code

## Graphiti API Endpoints (Confirmed LIVE)

- `POST /entity-node` - Create/upsert file or symbol entities
- `PATCH /nodes/{uuid}/summary` - Update existing node summaries
- `POST /api/tools/prune-missing` - Soft-delete removed files
- `GET /api/utils/uuid` - Deterministic UUID generation
- `GET /api/utils/edge-uuid` - Edge UUID generation
- `POST /messages` - Add narrative context (commit messages)

## Implementation Phases

1. GraphitiClient adapter (lib/GraphitiClient.js)
2. CodePerceptionWatcher service (lib/CodePerceptionWatcher.js)
3. Configuration and integration
4. Testing and validation

## Success Metrics

- Graph reflects code changes within <5 seconds of file save
- Agent can answer questions about new code immediately
- Zero race conditions during rapid editing

Huly Issue: HVSYN-910 ## Summary Replace hardcoded AGENTS.md template generation (currently split across LettaService.js and BeadsService.js) with a dedicated composable `AgentsMdGenerator` module using external templates and section registry. ## Problems Solved - Templates hardcoded inline in two different service files - Duplicate Beads CLI instructions in both templates - No way to customize per-project without editing source code - Every project gets identical instructions regardless of context ## Design Based on research of Clavix OSS patterns (DocInjector, marker-based blocks, config-driven adapters). ### Composable Sections

Section

Marker

Purpose

project-info

Project name, identifier, repo, filesystem path

beads-instructions

Beads CLI usage, issue lifecycle

session-completion

What to do when finishing a session

reporting-hierarchy

Who this agent reports to (PM agent)

Per-project overrides

### Architecture 1. **External template files** in `templates/` directory with variable interpolation 2. **Section registry** - config defining available sections, order, and defaults 3. **Per-project overrides** - global defaults + optional project-level config ### Out of Scope (v2) - Per-project tech-stack auto-detection - Auto-injecting framework-specific guidance ## Files Affected - NEW: `lib/AgentsMdGenerator.js` - NEW: `templates/` directory - MODIFIED: `lib/LettaService.js` (remove inline template) - MODIFIED: `lib/BeadsService.js` (remove inline template) ## Estimate ~4-6 hours

title: Add idle-data timeout to matrix-client SSE streaming
description:
Huly Issue: HVSYN-918

The matrix-client SSE streaming to Letta has no idle-data timeout. LETTA_STREAMING_TIMEOUT=3600s is a total timeout, but if the Letta agent hangs (sends keepalive pings but zero data chunks), the connection stays open indefinitely, locking the room for all subsequent messages. Need: (1) idle data timeout ~120s - if no real data chunk arrives for N seconds, cancel and unlock room. (2) Log the cancellation for tracking which agents hang. Root cause discovered during PM work loop testing - room was stuck for ~1h48m.

title: Add vendor/dependency exclusion filters to AST sync pipeline
description:
Huly Issue: HVSYN-917

## Problem

AST sync is pushing vendor/dependency files to Graphiti, creating ~23K noise nodes (~69% of GRAPH project nodes). This caused FalkorDB OOM crash (4G → 7.2G memory) and degrades search quality.

## Root Cause

No exclusion patterns in the file discovery or AST parsing pipeline. Every file in the project directory gets synced, including `node_modules/`, `vendor/`, `dist/`, etc.

## Solution

### 1. Add exclusion patterns to file discovery

Default excludes:

```
node_modules/
vendor/
static/vendor/
__pycache__/
.git/
dist/
build/
.next/
.nuxt/
*.min.js
*.bundle.js
*.map
```

### 2. Configurable via env var

`AST_EXCLUDE_PATTERNS` — comma-separated glob patterns appended to defaults.

### 3. Cleanup script

Provide a script or entity UUID list for purging existing vendor nodes from FalkorDB.

## Impact

- ~50% reduction in Graphiti memory usage
- Eliminates search noise from vendor code
- Fixes OOM risk at current scale

## Reported by

Graphiti KG team (cross-project)

title: Fix stuck SyncFromVibeWorkflow retrying 'Vibe task not found'

## Root Cause

Likely a Vibe Kanban task was deleted or its ID changed, but the workflow still holds the old reference. The retry policy has no terminal failure condition for 'not found' errors.

## Solution

- Add non-retryable error classification for 404/not-found responses in Vibe sync activities
- Terminate the stuck workflow via Temporal CLI or UI
- Add max retry cap or exponential backoff ceiling to prevent infinite retry loops

## Immediate Action

Terminate the stuck workflow manually.

LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder — strip teams' default blocks after spawn
description:
Discovered while smoke-testing vibesync-6wn and confirmed by user feedback (see feedback-letta-sdk-quirks memory).

## Why

Even with skipInit: true, letta-teams-sdk seeds 6 default memory blocks onto the spawned agent (anti-patterns, coordination-contract, completion-contract, operating-contract, execution-standards, identity). Role TOML blocks land alongside them, but role authorship over agent memory is incomplete.

Per user: don't fight the SDK; patch programmatically via the Letta REST API.

## Scope

Extend LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder.seed() (or a sibling method) with an optional `mode: 'replace' | 'augment'` parameter. In 'replace':
- After seeding the role-TOML blocks, list the agent's blocks via client.agents.blocks.list.
- For every label NOT in the role's memoryBlocks, call client.agents.blocks.detach(agentId, blockId) — DO NOT delete the block itself (other agents may reference it).
- Idempotent: re-running yields the same final block set.

Add a per-role TOML opt-in: `[[memory_blocks_policy]] mode = "replace"` defaults to 'augment' (today's behavior) for backwards compatibility.

## Tests

LettaTeamsMemoryBlockSeeder.test.ts grows a 'replace' case with a mocked client that lists 8 blocks, gets 2 from the role, and asserts 6 detach calls + 0 deletes.

## Done means

mode='replace' covered by unit tests; smoke test (vibesync-0h2.8 or a follow-up) verifies the role's agent ends up with EXACTLY the role-TOML's labels after spawn.

Related: feedback-letta-sdk-quirks (auto-memory).

title: Mid-run molecule cancellation — DELETE /molecules/:id
description:
Discovered while planning vibesync-0h2.

## Why

Once the dispatcher is wired through HTTP (vibesync-0h2.6), users will start formula runs they want to abort. The runtime layer supports it (runtime.tasks.cancel), but the dispatcher and the routes don't expose a cancel verb.

## Scope

- FormulaDispatcher.cancel(moleculeId): for every step bead in `running` state, call provider.stop on the active handle; walker.failStep with reason='cancelled'.
- DELETE /molecules/:id: 202 if dispatch is in-flight; 409 if already terminal.
- vibesync formula cancel <moleculeId> CLI verb.
- bus event: dispatcher/formula.cancelled.

## Tests

Run scripted to hang at message-delta; cancel mid-stream; assert step + molecule are marked cancelled; provider.stop called.

## Done means

Cancellation path covered by unit tests + a manual real-server check; documented in dispatcher + routes.

title: Dispatcher parallel ready-step fan-out
description:
Discovered while planning vibesync-0h2.

## Why

The MVP dispatcher (vibesync-0h2.3) processes ready steps serially even when MoleculeWalker.findReady() returns multiple. For formulas like code-review with strict sequential deps that's fine; for fan-out shapes (one root → three parallel reviewers → a merging step) it's a wasted opportunity.

## Scope

- Replace the `take first ready` loop with `Promise.all(ready.map(runStep))`.
- Cap concurrency via constructor option `maxParallelSteps` (default Infinity → respect only the dep graph).
- Per-teammate moleculeId still uniquifies the target name; no collision.
- Bus events keep their stepId so subscribers can interleave.

## Tests

Formula with two independent reviewer steps; assert both start before either finishes; outputs map correctly.

## Done means

parallel fan-out covered by unit tests; documented in dispatcher header.

title: Dispatcher resumability — re-attach to in-flight task after process restart
description:
Discovered while planning vibesync-0h2.

## Why

The MVP FormulaDispatcher (vibesync-0h2.3) walks steps in-process. If the service crashes or restarts mid-step, the molecule's running step bead stays in `running` state forever — the next process has no way to find out whether the Letta task ever finished. runtime.tasks.get(taskId) is the source of truth and persists across daemon restarts, but the dispatcher does not currently record the taskId on the step bead.

## Scope

- Persist taskId onto the step bead's metadata.exec at provider.prompt() time (in dispatcher.run(), before observe()).
- New FormulaDispatcher.resume(moleculeId): walks the molecule, finds steps in `running` state with a metadata.exec.task_id, re-attaches via runtime.tasks.get + a fresh observe() session, advances or fails as appropriate.
- Steps in `running` without a task_id (crashed BEFORE prompt) get failed with a clear reason.
- New CLI verb `vibesync formula resume <moleculeId>`.

## Tests

InMemoryDoltClient seeded with a `running` step + a task_id; FakeProvider scripted to return a recovered TaskState; assert resume completes the molecule.

## Done means

resume() works against the FakeProvider; documented in the dispatcher header comment with an example.

title: CLI verbs — vibesync formula {list,run,status,events}
description:
Thin HTTP client of the routes from .6. Mirrors the existing src/cli.ts pattern (commander + fetchJson).

## Scope

Extend src/cli.ts with a formula subcommand group:

  vibesync formula list
    → GET /formulas
    → pretty-prints a table: pack, name, description, stepCount.

  vibesync formula run <name> --input "<text>" [--pack gastown] [--motivating-bead <id>]
    → POST /formulas/<name>/run with the body
    → prints "moleculeId: <id>" then the bead URL/path.
    → with --watch, immediately chains into `events` for that moleculeId.

  vibesync formula status <moleculeId>
    → GET /molecules/<id>
    → prints step graph: stepName | role | status | output preview.

  vibesync formula events <moleculeId>
    → opens SSE connection to GET /molecules/<id>/events.
    → prints "{ts.toLocaleTimeString()} {kind} {compact-payload}" per event as they arrive.
    → exits 0 on dispatcher/formula.completed; exits 1 on dispatcher/formula.failed.
    → on Ctrl-C, closes the connection cleanly and exits 130.

Use undici for SSE (already a dependency). Reuse fetchJson for POST/GET.

## Tests (tests/unit/cli/formula.test.ts)

- list / run / status: assertions against a mock HTTP server that records the requests and returns canned responses.
- events: feed a recorded SSE stream, assert prints + exit code.

## Done means

- vibesync formula run code-review --input "review this diff" returns a moleculeId against a running ApiServer with orchestration enabled.
- vibesync formula events <moleculeId> streams to completion and exits 0.

title: Memory blocks for remaining gastown roles (coder/tester/mayor/refinery)
description:
Parallelizable TOML edit; no code change.

## Why

reviewer.toml has [[memory_blocks]] (persona + scope) added in commit b0b2d72; the other four don't. When .3's dispatcher spawns a teammate for one of those roles, roleConfig.memoryBlocks is undefined, so LettaTeamsProvider.start treats it as no-op and the agent runs on teams' default 6-block bundle alone. The role's intended persona/scope never reaches the agent.

## Scope

Mirror reviewer.toml's structure on:
- packs/gastown/roles/coder.toml
- packs/gastown/roles/tester.toml
- packs/gastown/roles/mayor.toml
- packs/gastown/roles/refinery.toml

For each, draft persona + scope blocks grounded in the existing packs/gastown/prompts/<role>-system.md (already on disk). Persona = one paragraph of "I am the X, I do Y, I prefer Z." Scope = one paragraph of "I do not do A, B; I delegate / refuse out-of-scope."

Limit fields: 2000 chars each (same as reviewer).

## Done means

- All five packs/gastown/roles/*.toml parse with memoryBlocks.length >= 2.
- bunx vitest run tests/unit/orchestration/packs passes (existing parser tests already exercise the path).
- No src/ changes.

## Parallelism

No dependency on other slices. Can be picked up anytime. .8 (smoke test) benefits from this landing first so the formula run exercises the seeder for all three code-review steps.
close_reason: Added persona/scope memory blocks to mayor, coder, tester, and refinery role TOML; pack unit and gastown integration tests pass with all roles carrying memory blocks.

title: Research — catalog Gas Town roles + behaviors for the gastown pack
description:

**Sources:**
- https://github.com/steveyegge/gastown (original)
- https://github.com/gastownhall/gascity/blob/main/docs/getting-started/coming-from-gastown.md (Gas City's migration guide)
- Steve Yegge's writing on the system

**Output:** docs/architecture/gastown-role-catalog.md with one section per role: name, purpose, model recommendation, key tools, sample system prompt.

This is the input the role-packs bead needs to ship actual TOML.

**Effort:** Half-day reading + writeup.
close_reason: Research doc landed at docs/architecture/gastown-role-catalog.md. Sourced from Gas City's coming-from-gastown.md migration guide. Catalog covers 7 Gas Town roles (Mayor, Deacon, Polecat, Witness, Refinery, Crew, Dog) with: purpose, model recommendation, key tools, system-prompt anchor, VibeSync mapping. Crucial framing: Gas City explicitly says role names are pack conventions, NOT SDK primitives — VibeSync inherits this discipline via layering invariant #5. Recommended pack contents for vibesync-ulp: ship mayor + refinery + reviewer/coder/tester + 3 formulas + 5 prompt templates. Do NOT ship deacon (HealthPatrol covers it), polecat (a pattern not a role), witness (defer), dog (mostly exec orders not LLM).

title: Role packs — Gas Town role catalog as a VibeSync pack
description:
**Sequencing step 11** — "build Gas Town in VibeSync" explicitly.

Author Gas Town's role catalog (Mayor, Deacon, Polecat, etc.) as VibeSync teammate configs in a pack. Validates the formula + molecule + RuntimeProvider abstractions against a real, mature role catalog.

**Pack structure** (mirrors Gas City's `internal/builtinpacks/`):

```
packs/gastown/
  pack.toml
  roles/
    mayor.toml
    deacon.toml
    polecat.toml
    ...
  formulas/
    onboard-feature.toml
    code-review.toml
    ...
  prompts/
    mayor-system.md
    deacon-system.md
    ...
```

**Why this matters:**
- Validates the abstractions against a real role catalog
- Surfaces gaps early
- Provides a reference customers can fork

**Non-goals:**
- Don't put role names in core code (layering invariant #5)
- Don't claim 1:1 fidelity with original Gas Town behavior — shape + role catalog, not a port

**Acceptance:**
- Pack ships in packs/gastown/ with ≥3 roles + 1 formula
- `vibesync pack install gastown` registers the roles
- README pointer documenting the pack

**Effort:** Medium. Role config + system prompt authoring is the substance.

**Depends on:** Pack mechanism (vibesync-* "Pack mechanism..."), formulas (vibesync-* "Formulas..."), molecules (vibesync-* "Molecules..."), Gas Town research (vibesync-* "Research Gas Town...").

Manifest + 5 role TOMLs (mayor, reviewer, coder, tester, refinery) + 3 formula TOMLs (code-review, onboard-feature, refinery-sweep) + 5 prompt templates. Pack discovery framework (vibesync-lhn) loads it cleanly. Roles intentionally omitted per role-catalog research: deacon (HealthPatrol covers it), polecat (pattern not role), witness (defer), dog (mostly exec orders). 8/8 integration tests green: validatePath, manifest+roles+formulas, role tools+prompts wiring, all three formula chains, discovery scope discipline. title: ACP runtime provider — speak Agent Client Protocol (JSON-RPC stdio)
description:
**Sequencing step 10** — open VibeSync to third-party agent dispatch.

ACP (Agent Client Protocol) is the JSON-RPC-over-stdio protocol Gas City uses for its `acp` runtime provider. Adding ACP support lets VibeSync dispatch to any ACP-compliant agent — opening the door to non-Letta agents.

**Reference impl:** /opt/stacks/gascity/internal/runtime/acp/ — protocol.go (envelope), conn.go (lifecycle), acp.go (provider).

**Methods to support:**
- `initialize` (handshake with capability negotiation)
- `session/new` (cwd, MCP servers)
- `session/prompt` (send user input as content blocks)
- `session/update` (server-emitted streaming updates — notification, no response)

**Why P3 not P2:**
- VibeSync's primary clients are Letta agents (LettaPMAgentProvider) and letta-teams-sdk teammates (LettaTeamsProvider); neither needs ACP
- ACP unlocks third-party agent compatibility but isn't on the critical path
- Promote if/when a customer asks for non-Letta agent support

**Acceptance:**
- ACPProvider round-trips initialize/session/new/session/prompt against a fake ACP server
- One test against an actual ACP-compliant agent (Gas City's `gc` binary works)

**Depends on:** RuntimeProvider (vibesync-* "Define RuntimeProvider...").
close_reason: ACPProvider at src/orchestration/runtime/acp-provider.ts. TS port of Gas City's internal/runtime/acp/ client side. spawn(command, args, cwd); JSON-RPC 2.0 envelope construction (initialize, initialized, session/new, session/prompt, session/update). SKELETON: handshake response correlation + stdout line-buffering deferred; promote priority when a third-party ACP customer arrives. Reference impl at /opt/stacks/gascity/internal/runtime/acp/ for full lifecycle.

title: Add Dolt time-travel to recent_activity block builder
description:
Use Dolt's AS OF syntax to build the recent_activity memory block by diffing current state against a past commit (e.g., 24h ago or last N commits).

## Approach
- Find commit hash closest to 24h ago: dolt log --oneline | filter by date
- Query: SELECT * FROM dolt_diff('issues', commit_24h_ago, 'HEAD')
- Classify changes: created (added rows), updated (modified rows), closed (status→closed)
- Feed into buildRecentActivity() or replace it entirely

This eliminates the need for interactions.jsonl parsing and gives accurate change tracking.

## Files
- Modify: lib/LettaMemoryBuilders.js (buildRecentActivity)
- Or: lib/DoltQueryService.js (add getRecentChanges method)

Parent: vibesync-6tv
Depends on: vibesync-pqi

title: Research Vibe Kanban parent-child task model
notes:
Findings (2026-02-07):
- Vibe task model exposes parent_workspace_id (workspace/attempt FK), not a direct parent_task_id by Huly identifier.
- Task create/update APIs accept parent_workspace_id, but this requires a known workspace UUID from Vibe runtime state.
- Sync-created tasks may not have immediately usable workspace mapping for deterministic cross-system parent linking.
Conclusion: robust cross-system parent tracking should use explicit metadata on task description (Huly Parent: <ID|none>) and reconcile to Huly from that canonical marker.
close_reason: Implemented/validated in current sync batch (parent-child + reparenting + realtime Beads tests + smoke coverage).

title: Research Huly sub-issue API and data model
notes:
Findings (2026-02-07):
- Huly supports explicit parent-child operations in API client surface:
  - create sub-issue via POST /issues/{parentIdentifier}/subissues
  - reparent/top-level move via PATCH /issues/{issueIdentifier}/parent with parentIdentifier (nullable)
- Current sync paths can apply parent changes through moveIssue / setParentIssue / MCP fallback.
- Parent payloads may appear as object (parentIssue.identifier) or legacy string; sync now normalizes both forms.
Conclusion: Huly side supports create-subissue + reparent + move-to-top-level semantics required for bidirectional sync.
close_reason: Implemented/validated in current sync batch (parent-child + reparenting + realtime Beads tests + smoke coverage).

title: Concurrent dispatcher rate limits — cap N in-flight molecules per process
description:
Discovered while planning vibesync-0h2.

## Why

The MVP dispatcher (vibesync-0h2.3) has no concurrency cap at the FormulaDispatcher level. Each dispatcher.run() consumes a teammate slot on the Letta server and (if .4-deferred lands) some N tokens. Without a cap, a misbehaving caller (or a test loop) can saturate the daemon.

## Scope

- FormulaDispatcher gains an internal semaphore (default Infinity, configurable via constructor option `maxConcurrentMolecules`).
- Excess run() calls queue (FIFO) rather than reject.
- bus events: dispatcher/formula.queued with depth + position.
- Surface depth via GET /formulas (response.queue?.depth).

## Tests

run() called 5 times with cap=2; assert 2 in-flight, 3 queued; advance fake clock; assert FIFO.

## Done means

Rate limit covered by tests; documented in dispatcher header; threshold tunable per environment.

title: Dispatcher retry / backoff policy on step failure
description:
Discovered while planning vibesync-0h2.

## Why

The MVP dispatcher (vibesync-0h2.3) fails the molecule on the first step error. Real flows want at least one retry for transient failures (model overloaded, network blips, tool-call hiccups).

## Scope

- Add a per-step retry policy field to the formula TOML schema: `retries = N` (default 0), `retry_backoff_ms = M` (default 1000).
- On step error, dispatcher consults the policy: if attempts < retries, sleep backoff and re-prompt the same handle (or re-start a fresh teammate, depending on what the SDK supports cleanly). Increment attempts on the bead metadata.
- bus events: dispatcher/step.retry with attempt # + reason.
- Cap total wall time per step to prevent infinite loops.

## Tests

FakeProvider that fails on attempts 1-2 and succeeds on 3; verify step finishes; assert exec.metadata.attempts === 3.

## Done means

Retry policy documented in the formula TOML schema; covered by unit tests; the smoke test grows a retry case once .8 is green.
